The suggested answers presented below inevitably give much more detail than would be expected from most candidates under examination conditions,and include most of the obvious points evidenced from the case information. The answers are therefore intended to provide a structure of the approach required from candidates,and cover the range and depth of knowledge relating to each task which might be demonstrated by the most well prepared and able candidates.They are also intended to support revision and tuition for future examinations. 1 (a)To:Business development director of PVS Briefing notes on the potential joint venture between PVS and Best4Pets These briefing notes will provide an assessment of the potential joint venture between PVS and Best4Pets and recommend whether a franchise agreement would be better than a joint venture. Impact on the PVS brand The clinics are to be located in Best4Pets stores in the north and east of Arland whereas PVS clinics are in the south of Arland. Provided the clinics are PVS branded,the collaboration would therefore spread the PVS brand geographically. The vast majority of Best4Pets customers are likely to be pet owners.Given the growth in their customer numbers,which is in excess of the growth in pet ownership,a large and increasing proportion of pet owners will encounter the PVS brand should clinics open in Best4Pets stores. The joint venture should therefore increase awareness of the PVS brand. Delivery of service features(locaton,innovation and quality of service) Parking facilities at,and public transport links to,Best4Pets stores make the stores,and hence the proposed clinics,easy to access,which is important to clients. Best4Pets stores are not in town centres,which have proved a popular location for PVS clinics.With traffic congestion increasing in town centres,however,and Best4Pets stores being easy to access,the stores may be a preferred location for clients. The collaboration between PVS and Best4Pets is the first of its kind in the industry and would demonstrate and re-establish PVS's ability to be innovative and it would add to PVS's overall service offering. The environment of Best4Pets's budget stores is likely to be at odds with PVS's welcoming and comfortable clinics.The culture of Best4Pets is also likely to be very different to that of PVS.The culture of PVS is one driven by values of help,support and understanding for both internal and external stakeholders.Given Best4Pets's values and their focus on external success, their culture is likely to prioritise growth,profitability and meeting targets.The way in which this may be reflected in thee environment and layout of stores,promotional and marketing activity and attitudes of staff-who may be under pressure to upsell aggressively-may not align with the quality of service PVS aims to deliver.The basic level of customer service offered in Best4Pets's stores is likely to impact on the perception of the quality of service offered in the superstore clinics.For instance, should clinic clients shop in the store before or after a clinic visit,the service encountered is likely to be at odds with the level of service PVS attempts to provide.Potential clients looking for a high-quality service may therefore be discouraged from using the PVS clinics in store. The joint venture would therefore deliver the location and innovation service features but there is a risk that the perception of the quality of service being offered in the clinics would be less than the level PVS would want to see delivered. Franchising Both parties to a joint venture tend to share set-up and running costs,financial risks and rewards.This reduces PVS's exposure in relation to the costs of setting up the clinics and the overallfinancial risk to PVS of an unsuccessful collaboration. A comprehensive legal contract underpinning the arrangement would help to minimise risks.Disagreements over profit sharing and management of a joint venture are common,however,and these might be exacerbated by the different cultures of the two parties. Under a franchise arrangement,Best4Pets would pay a capital lump sum to PVS for rights to use PVS's name,goodwill and systems and for subsequent support services which PVS may supply.They would also bear the day-to-day running costs and staffing of the new clinics.If a clinic failed,Best4Pets would bear the brunt of the failure.Franchising therefore significantly reduces the capital outlay and financial risks compared with those associated with a joint venture. However,under a franchise agreement,PVS would have far less control over the operation of the clinics and over its brand than if a joint venture were established.The clinics would be PVS branded and so PVS would need to impose a range of strict quality control measures on Best4Pets to ensure that an appropriate level of service was provided and to minimise any negative impact on PVS's brand.Given the different cultures of the two organisations and Best4Pets lack of experience in the running of vet clinics,Best4Pets may struggle to provide the level of service deemed necessary. Franchising would reduce financial risks but could negatively impact brand via poor levels of service and therefore it is recommended that a joint venture arrangement is undertaken. Al-driven analysis can deliver more accurate test results more quickly than traditional methods,which allows pets to be treated at a lower cost.Any cost savings passed on to clients via lower prices could result in growth in market share. There is a shortage of staffacross the industry and staff recruitment and retention,which is on the risk register,impacts PVS's ability to grow.Telehealth could be merged with the current mode of operating and used to treat patients.It could also be used to triage patients.Staf time could therefore be used more efficiently,with vet nurses dealing with minor ailments,reducing thee working hours of veterinarians.Telehealth could also enable staff to carry out online consultations from home.Such changes to ways of operating could provide a better work-life balance,reduce stress,and improve staff wellbeing.This could potentially lead to a reduction in staff turnover and,by positioning PVS as an attractive employer,help attract staff.With appropriatee staffing levels,PVS would be in a better position to grow. The convenience of telehealth-of not necessarily having to have a face-to-face consultation but being able to have one at home -would be a huge benefit for many clients,as would improvements in communication such as being able to book consultations online.Telehealth also widens the geographical area from which clients are likely to be drawn.Online consultations mean that clients may not necessarily choose the closest clinic but rather the one they believe to be 'best.These changes delivered by technology could attract new clients and help retain existing ones. A digital marketing strategy would provide PVS with more effective marketing to enable growth.There would also be improvements in client engagement using social media,making clients more loyal and improving retention. Slide 2 The drawbacks for PVS,including ethical issues,of adopting the technological developments suggested Capital investment required Risk of obsolescence of technologies used Affordability of many technologically-advanced treatments Ethical issues associated with the use of Al Impact on staff wellbeing and potential ethical implications Negative effect on client retention Risk of hacking and cybercrime and ethical concerns associated with the use of data Many technological developments such as that required to produce prosthetics using 3D printing may require capital investment which may be greater than PVS's funding abilities or in excess of an amount PVS may want to borrow. There is also a risk of obsolescence withall technology-a new technology could be developed which makes the existing one and the associated treatments redundant. Prices charged for many technologically-advanced treatments are likely to have to be higher than those for standard treatments so that PVS can recover the costs of any investment.PVS does of course have its Healthcare Plan,which may prove useful for clients who want to take advantage of the benefits of more expensive treatment. Ethical concerns have been raised about the use of Al.These include the potential for Al to result in employees losing their jobs and lead to wealth inequality because it is likely to have a disproportionate impact on those in lower-paid roles whose jobs are easier to replace with Al technology.It has the potential to invade or violate privacy and it can make mistakes.Finally,it can discriminate against some sections of the population-any bias in the data used to train machines will be reflected in thee algorithms used.These concerns could impact on PVS's reputation. Technology could impact both staff and clients adversely.The need to become familiar with technology and the delivery of online consultations which lack face-to-face engagement with clients and patients may further affect the wellbeing of staff.To knowingly pursue a strategy for growth which could potentially negatively impact staff's wellbeing may be perceived as being at odds with our honesty and integrity value and potentially unethical. Clients may feel that,with less interaction with vet staff because of telehealth,a lower level of service is being provided, impacting retention. Should PVS decide to invest in digital marketing,there would be a need to ensure controls were in place to protect against hacking,cybercrime and theft of client data held online.Ethical concerns have been raised about the use of client data and the privacy,sensitivity and security of this data.The costs of adequate protection would need to be considered against thee reputational and potentially legal consequences of a breach in security. 2 (a)To:The human resources(HR)director of PVS Briefing notes on corporate social responsibility(CSR)as a growth strategy These briefing notes set out the extent to which a CSR strategy is a growth strategy by considering its impact on PVS's key stakeholders. Staff Socially-responsibleinitiatives with respect to staff could focus on improving their health and wellbeing.This could include allowing staff to engage in community or volunteering projects,ensuring a supportive and kind working environment, encouraging a better work-life balance and making staf feel valued and respected.According to PVS's website,the organisation already has strategies in place to ensure fair pay and fair treatment,to stamp out discrimination and encourage diversity.Its values of honesty and integrity and quality and care support a CSR agenda with respect to staff.However,you will e in my emailto you concerning the project manager job description,some staff may not be aware of these strategies.It may therefore be appropriate to conduct some training across the organisation on PVS's stance on diversity and discrimination. PVS will therefore save costs in relation to recruitment of staff-as staff retention will improve-and staff absence-as wellbeing will improve.The resulting improvements in productivity,pet care and client care will help retain existing clients and attract new ones,thereby supporting growth. Clients PVS's competitors are not currently engaged in CSR and hence adopting a CSR strategy should give PVS a competitive advantage.A publicised focus on CSR and the initiatives taking place will help retain and attract clients who are among thee 70%of Arland consumers who want to know the brands they are supporting address social and environmental issues.Whilst 'greenwashing'can impact on client retention,and PVS will need to be seen to deliver on its CSR commitments,PSV's values of honesty and integrity will signal that it is an ethical business which will not take advantage of clients or recommend treatments and medication which their pets do not need.This will help to attract and retain clients looking for a business acting with integrity and a commitment to ethical business,leading to growth. Given that public attention continues to grow on both the way in which an organisation interacts with its stakeholders and its impact on the environment and that consumers increasingly want to interact with socially-and environmentally-responsible organisations,it may be that PVS needs to implement a CSR strategy not just to grow but to ensure long-term survival. CCVP The additional requirements for the Higher level of SPP accreditation awarded by the CCVP requires organisations to do more to benefit saff,patients,clients,the community and the environment.By adopting a CSR strategy,PVS will therefore be more likely to gain this level of accreditation,which only 5%of pet vet organisations hold.This could give PVS yet another competitive advantage-new clients would be attracted by the assurance of good patient and client care,supporting growth. AVU The AVU is increasingly concerned about the wellbeing of its members.Should the AVU publicly condemn PVS for the treatment of its staff,the ensuing negative publicity would undermine the PVS brand and clients could choose to use competitors. Initiatives to address AVU and staff concerns about wellbeing should reduce the possibility of negative publicity.This may not lead to growth but should protect against the loss of clients. Local community Pet owners tend to go to the vet clinic closest to where they live.Community initiatives such as support for local animal charities willimprove overall community engagement,help spread the PVS brand locally and hence attract more clients. Shareholders Whilst the impact of a CSR strategy on the shareholders (the board and the founder)is unlikely to be linked to growth,they may face a conflict of interest.Some may be keen to protect their dividends rather than see PVS contributing to the wellbeing of communities and society through environmental and social measures. Whilst it is true that CSR activities will require some level of investment,the amount is unlikely to be significant.Further analysis should be carried out to determine in more detail the costs and financial benefits of the suggested initiatives.CSR is more likely to increase profit and therefore potentially dividends,and support the growth of the organisation. (b)Email To:HR director From:External consultant Date:4 June 20X4 Subject:Draft job description for project manager Dear HR director I trust this email finds you well. Further to our conversation,I have reviewed the draft job description for a project manager which has been prepared by thee junior HR assistant. The following points should be noted: Reporting lines A project manager,even one working on projects which are a direct result of the CEO's demand for growth,would not usually report to the CEO but would report instead to the project sponsor.The project sponsor is often the individual with the most invested in the project,with the most to gain from its success or lose from its failure.In this case,the project sponsor might be the executive director who proposed the strategy for which the project has been established.The job of the project sponsor is to direct the project,while the job of the project manager is to manage the project.A strong link between the sponsor and manager is therefore necessary.If PVS was a small organisation,however,this reporting structure may be inappropriate and various project roles might be merged. Job overview The job overview is that for a project sponsor.It is the project sponsor who is ultimately accountable or answerable to the board for the delivery of projects.The project manager is responsible for its delivery;they carry out the tasks which deliver the project. The project sponsor is also the point of contact between the board and the project team.The project manager willupdate the project sponsor on progress,who will then update the board. It should be made clear that the projects are strategic projects to ensure a project manager with experience of working at a strategic level is recruited. Responsibilities The project manager would be responsible for developing a detailed project plan and managing projects on a day-to-day basis, as well as creating and maintaining project documentation.They would also be responsible for identifying and managing thee project risks(in conjunction with the project team and potentially the sponsor),whereas the project sponsor is concerned about the overall riskiness of the project and how it aligns to the risk appetite of PVS. The project manager would not appoint the sponsor (although the sponsor may be involved in the appointment of the project manager).The sponsor is likely to be someone relatively senior within the organisation(as noted above,potentially a member of the executive team)and so it would be inappropriate for the appointment to be made by the project manager.The project manager may be involved in the appointment of the project team.They will be responsible for building,motivating and facilitating the project team but may not be managing them directly.Members of the project team may continue to be managed by their functional managers whilst working on a project.Project team members tend to be drawn from across the organisation as the team needed will depend on the skills required for the project.They will then return to their functional areas once the project is complete. Project managers do need to carry out monitoring and control activities to ensure projects are delivered on time and on budget, but those activities also need to ensure projects are delivered to scope-in other words,the tasks required to fulfil the project's goals have been completed.Ideally the board will want the projects to finish on time,within budget and to produce the required deliverables but often this is not possible and the project manager needs to balance these triple constraints,making trade-offs as necessary depending on the nature of the project. Skills and experience A project manager must be able to work on their own,meet deadlines,monitor and control budgets and motivate others but there are a range of other skills which could be needed including:leadership skills (which is more than just being able to motivate others);the ability to work as part of a team;effective communication skills so as to be able to engage effectively with all project stakeholders;risk management skills;negotiation skills(needed to negotiate with suppliers,team members and other stakeholders,particularly when balancing the triple constraints). There is no absolute requirement for a project manager to have experience of the vet industry as project management skills have universal applicability.Some knowledge would be an advantage,however,as the project manager would be more aware of industry terminology,rules and regulations such as those set by the CCVP and the current challenges in the industry such as the wellbeing of staff.Ten years'experience would seem excessive,however. Other points The expectation that the role holder will work unpaid overtime,including at evenings and weekends,would not seem to align to PVS's values of honesty and integrity and quality and care.Whilst I am unclear on the exact terms of employment for the role,I would advise that something which appears to undermine these values should not be included and more appropriate wording such as The role holder may be expected to work some overtime,occasionally at evenings and weekends'should be considered. The PVS website also sets out clearly that PVS operates a non-discrimination policy;the statement that mothers with young children should not apply for the role is discriminatory and must therefore be removed as this is in direct contradiction of it.As I noted in my briefing notes about the CSR strategy,it may be appropriate to conduct training across the organisation on PVS's stance on diversity and discrimination. The final statement in the job description should also be removed.This undermines the values of honesty and integrity and could be deemed highly unethical;it appears to be implying that PVS will give preference to candidates who are willing to share confidential,business-sensitive information about pet vet organisations where they might have worked.Indeed,in some instances this may beillegal,for example,if the employee's contract provides adequate protection for confidential information such that the employee is in breach of confidentiality or if data protection legislation has been broken. I hope this information proves useful.Please do not hesitate to get in touch if you need further clarification. 3 Report To:Chief finance officer(CFO) From:External consultant Date:6 June 20X4 Subject:Internal control weaknesses in the credit control department Introduction This report assesses the implications of the internal control weaknesses in the credit control department which are set out in the report prepared by the internal audit team and recommends appropriate actions to mitigate those weaknesses. Implications Weakness 1 If reviews are not undertaken regularly,and there is no formal timetable for the collection of receivables,cash flow is likely to be adversely affected.Cash could come in later than planned,and the overall amount received may be less than anticipated.This could affect day-to-day operations if PVS is unable to pay staff or suppliers,it increases the cost of working capital and could ultimately lead to the failure of the business.Bad debts will have to be written off,impacting profit. Client satisfaction may also be affected by what they perceive as poor service if,for example,reminders that payments are due arrive late.This could affect client numbers and cause bad publicity and devalue the PVS brand. Weakness 2 A lack of protection on the PVS servers means that client details,including sensitive information,is at risk from external hackers and abuse from dishonest staff.In addition,there is a risk from computer viruses,which could corrupt and render the information useless.The risks posed by hackers and viruses raise legal considerations because the stolen or corrupted data would relate to individuals and PVS could face legal action because of the insuficient protection over the data.The negative publicity from a data breach or virus could impact on client numbers and negatively affect PVS's brand.Potential clients may not believe that their data is safe;a loss of a sense of security can drive clients-both potential and existing-elsewhere. The loss of information (or its integrity or its confidentiality)could lead to business disruption and loss of time and money. Weakness 3 The implications of the lack of training include angry clients,not just those who have been affected directly from the lack of training but those who are affected by the backlog of work in the department.These clients may take their custom elsewhere and cause bad publicity for PVS.In addition,overworked and potentally stressed staff may leave PVS,exacerbating the staff turnover isue and resulting in the need for even more temporary staff (which could lead to more irate clients,and more work for staff,and so on).It may also become increasingly difficult to hire temporary staff,meaning that any backlog of work could increase. The mistakes themselves would be impacting on cash flow-there will be a delay in payment of direct debits and invoices if clients are not set up correctly and invoices are incorrect.As noted above,this can affect day-to-day operations and could potentially impact on the long-term survival of the business. Weakness 4 Given that no documentation setting out the Plan details is provided to clients,they must rely on information provided by clinic reception staf.PVS will therefore have to honour the three-month deferral of payment otherwise there is a risk of alienating clients and causing bad publicity.The change to a deferal of one month was presumably made to improve cash flow,but there will now be a lag in that improvement. Weakness 5 Clients who have not kept up direct debit payments and/or have overdue invoices could be less likely to pay new invoices,affecting cash flow and adding to the level of bad debts and the amount which potentially may need to be written offagainst profit.Such clients also add to the work of the credit control department,which is already suffering from staff shortages which are causing problems as noted above. Mitigations Weakness 1 A formal timetable should be established and implemented as soon as possible.This should set out timings for invoicing,sending reminders,chasing payments,involving a debt collection agency and starting legal action to recover a debt.Such timings should ensure that cash flow meets the needs of the business and does not have too high a cost in terms of any overdrafts or loans which PVS may have. Al taff involved in the process should be fully aware of this timetable.Any exceptions to the timetable for a particular client should be authorised by the credit control department manager. The reviews should be conducted on a regular basis-for example,weekly-by the credit control department manager to provide necessary insights into the financial health of PVS(such as an understanding of the proportion of receivables balances unlikely to be received)and the effectiveness of the credit control department's debt management practices. Weakness 2 Security of the data needs to be increased with immediate effect.To prevent unauthorised access from staff,access controls should be put in place.These ensure only authorised staff are granted access to the relevant part of the system or to specific programs or files,and include passwords and user names.Multiple passwords could be required for particularly sensitive information. Controls to prevent unauthorised external access should also be used.These could include firewalls,data encryption and virus protection. Weakness 3 An investigation into the reason for the high staff urnover rate in the department should be caried out and action taken to address any issues.It may be that the volume of work has increased but no extra staff resource has been made available.Permanent staff should therefore be recruited(and trained appropriately).The lack of process in the department may have made the job ificult. The implementation of the timetable mentioned above should mitigate this to some extent. Training of temporary staf should be improved and an immediate focus of training for existing temporary staff should be on the two issues identified-setting up new clients on the Plan and producing accurate invoices. Weakness 4 There should be a formal means of communicating changes to the Plan to clinic reception staff to ensure that they are aware of them in advance of their coming into effect.Depending on the nature of the changes and their frequency,this might be via email,via the intranet or via online or face-to-face training(which might be appropriate if turnover of reception staff is high). In addition,Plan documentation should be produced for clients,kept up to date and be provided to all clients signing up to the Plan so that they are aware of the terms and conditions.Plan terms and conditions should be clearly displayed in receptions,with key elements highlighted. A clear statement of terms and conditions on invoices will also help ensure clients are aware of them. Weakness 5 With the implementation of a formal timetable over the credit control process,and regular reviews,clients who have payments in arrears will be identified early in the process,at which point they should be added to a'stoplwatch list'or a notification or alert put on their Plan record which ensures that clinic reception staff do not continue to offer deferred payment to such clients. In addition,a check on clients should be carried out before they are signed up to the Plan to ensure they have not previously been a member of the Plan and been removed due to unpaid invoices. Summary If you require any further information on the implications of the weaknesses identified or need further advice on appropriate mitigations,please do not hesitate to get in touch. PVS website extract 10 1.Introduction Praecuro Vet Services Ltd(PVS)provides pet veterinary (vet)services in Arland.It is the fifth largest organisation in the industry in Arland and has been established for 32 years. 2.Pet vet services industry-Arland Overview The pet vet services industry provides healthcare for pets.The organisations within the industry employ specialist animal doctors,known as veterinarians,and specialist animal nurses (vet nurses)to deliver veterinary (vet)services in clinics. Pets are domesticated or tamed animals which are kept primarily as a source of companionship or pleasure.They include cats and dogs as well as other small mammals,some birds,small reptiles and some types of rodent. Veterinarians study veterinary medicine at university for five years,take demanding exams and undergo rigorous on-the-job training. Services provided Services provided by vet clinics for pet owners (clients)and their pets(patients)include: 一 Annual vaccinations 一 Diagnosis of health conditions and diseases 一 Medical treatment once a condition,disease or injury has been diagnosed - Prescription of medication.A prescription is an authorisation by the veterinarian for the issue of certain medication 一 Sale of both prescription and non-prescription medication 一 Routine surgical operations 一 Referrals for serious medical issues,complex surgery,or specialist services 一 Guidance and advice on pet care 一 Sale of specialist pet food which addresses specific health and age-related issues 一 Sale of other products ('accessories')such as bedding,cages and toys for pets These services are usually provided via consultations with a veterinarian or a vet nurse. Consultations are usually booked on the telephone or online.In Arland,the average consultancy time is 15 minutes. Market structure Traditionally,vet organisations were small businesses run by veterinarians or a group of veterinarians.Each business included one ortwo clinics.Growth was driven slowlyand organically by obtaining new clients. As the number of pets in Arland increased,the size of many businesses grew.Larger organisations began acquiring smaller ones due to the potential cost savings from economies of scale.It is anticipated that this acquisition activity willcontinue over the next five years. More recently,multinational organisations,particularly those which have pet food divisions,have been attracted by the industry's profits and have been acquiring vet businesses.An added attraction of the industry is that it provides another route to market for their pet food products. These multinational organisations are expected to continue their expansion through acquisition activities. Currently,three multinational organisations control 52%of the Arland pet vet services industry. They operate clinics on a nationwide basis.There are also three large,independent vet organisations which own and operate clinics ona regional basis.Hundreds of smaller businesses, usually employing less than ten people,operate locally. Market share (by revenue)of major players in the Arland pet vet services industry on 31 March 20X4 Name Gloas Group Bellstone Group Hude Group Dennis Vet Clinics Praecuro Vet Services Good Health Veterinarians Other Arland market share 23% 16% 13% 7% 6% 4% 31% On 31 March 20X4,the Arland pet vet services industry employed almost 36,000 people and generated revenue of $2·5 billion. Industry outlook in Arland Revenue Average annual growth in revenue in pet vet services over the last five years has been 3·5%,but growth levels of 6·8%are expected for the next five years.Levels of pet ownership have been increasing for a decade and are expected to increase at even faster rates over the next five years,thereby increasing demand for services.In addition,advances in pet nutrition,diagnosis, treatment and care are leading to longer pet lifespans. Revenue growth is also expected from pet healthcare plans,which are increasing in popularity. These spread the cost of treatment pets may need over several months,making complex treatment more affordable. Changing attitudes to pets is also having a positive impact on revenue.Clients are generally willing to spend increasing amounts of money taking care of their pets.Most pet owners consider the good health of pets to be a necessity and use the services of veterinarians regardless of their disposable income. Costs Staff costs,including training,comprise the biggest proportion of the costs incurred by a pet vet organisation.Purchases include medical supplies such as medication,bandages,and syringes as well as products purchased for resale(pet food and accessories).Other costs include premises,administration,health,and safety(for example in relation to the storage of medicine) and marketing. Clients Whilst historically pets have tended to be owned by those over the age of 35,60%of new pet owners in Arland in the last five years are aged 16 to 34. Clients choose clinics which they can travel to easily so that they can get there quickly in an emergencyand so that they can minimise transportation time for theirpets.Clients are increasingly looking for convenient consultation times -in evenings and at weekends -meaning pet vet organisations are under pressure to extend opening hours. Many clients benchmark consultation fees charged by organisations in the same area.In order to keep fees low,many vet organisations mark up medication prices rather than increase consultation fees. Challenges within the industry in Arland Recruitment and retention of staff is becoming increasingly difficult.There is an increasing demand for veterinarians because of the recent rise in the number of pets in Arland,and fewer people are training as veterinarians because of the rising cost of the training.These two factors have led to a nationwide shortage of veterinarians. Long working hours -driven by a shortage of staff,a sharp rise in pet ownership and client demands for a 24 hours a day,7 days a week,365 days a year (24/7/365)service-are an increasing problem across the industry.Challenging negotiations with pet owners who cannot afford treatment are a regular occurrence.These two issues are having an effect on the wellbeing of staff.Across the industry,5%of staff are absent with work-related stress and staff retention is problematic,with average staff turnover at 13%. Client loyalty is low.There is a high level of competition on fees,quality of care,range of services offered and opening hours between clinics which are located close to each other. Although competition has fallen to some extent over the past few years because of industry consolidation,competition from other channels is increasing as follows: Source of competition Large,budget pet superstores Online pet medication providers Telehealth Services provided Two superstores(Best4Pets and Pet Fare)sell a wide range of pet food and pet accessories at low prices.Store staff(who do not have vet training)also offer general advice about pets. If clients obtain prescriptions from their veterinarians,they can buy prescription medication,often at a significant discount to the prices charged by pet vet organisations,from online pet medication providers. Telehealth uses electronic communication and information technologies to enable healthcare professionals to provide healthcare services remotely(at a distance).There are several pet vet telehealth providers in Arland offering services to their own clients or on behalf of other organisations.Some only offer remote services such as advice 24/7/365 via telephone, webchat,or video consultations,while others provide a mix of both remote and face-to-face services. Pet healthcare has a large carbon footprint due to waste production,resource consumption and medicines usage.There have been recent negative press reports on the human healthcare industry which highlight the need to consider the environmental impact of that industry and there are fears within the pet vet services industry that it will soon face similar negative publicity. Innovation and developments Technology is often recognised as one of the main challenges facing the pet vet services industry. Technological advances in human healthcare,such as the use of artificial intelligence and machine learning,are expected to continue to filter down into more advanced medical,surgical, and diagnostic pet vet procedures.Many developments have made the industry more capital intensive but have led to an increase in the quality and breadth of services which veterinarians can provide.Some of these new and improved services are expensive to deliver,but technology has also reduced some consultation times meaning pets can be treated at a lower cost to clients. Technology has also had a favourable impact on both the business management software used by the industry and communication with clients.Digital technology is being increasingly used by the Gloas Group,Bellstone Group and Hude Group to market their services more effectively. Telehealth is providing increased flexibility and convenience for clients. Industry bodies,regulations and legislation Veterinarians must register with the Chartered College of Vet Professionals (CCVP)which regulates them,supports the public interest and safeguards pets.The CCVP's code of conduct sets out the professional responsibilities of veterinarians with respect to patients (the pet),clients CCVPSPPAccredited Organisation(the pet owner),the profession and the public. CCVP SPP Accredited Organisation The CCVP runs the Standards for Pets Programme(SPP).Most of Arland's pet vet organisations have the Core level of SPP accreditation but only 5%hold the Higher level.The Higher level requires organisations to show how they have achieved extra considerations which benefit the vet team, patients,clients,the community and the environment. Many veterinarians and vet nurses are members of the Arland Vet Union (AVU),which campaigns for better workplace conditions across the industry.The AVU is increasingly concerned about the wellbeing of its members. Organisations within the pet vet services industry have several legal obligations.For example: - Compliance with specific health and safety legislation to protect staff and the general public from,for example,pet-inflicted injuries such as bites. - Requirement to display a large notice informing clients that they can request a written prescription.This means the client does not need to buy the medication at the clinic treating their pet but can source it elsewhere,potentially more cheaply,should they want to. Provision of,or pay another organisation to provide on their behalf,a 24/7/365 emergency service. 3.Praecuro Vet Services Ltd Background Praecuro Vet Services Ltd(PVS)is the fifth largest pet vet organisation inArland.It was established 32 years ago by Pietr Praecuro.Most of its growth has been organic,although under the current board and senior management it has acquired a few small independent businesses which have been successfully integrated into the organisation. PVS runs 168 clinics in the south of Arland.Clinics vary in size,some having one or two veterinarians,others having nine or ten.PVS's head office,which is also based in the south of Arland,provides finance,IT,purchasing,legal,facilities management and human resources functions. PVS has a total of 2,490 staff -860 veterinarians,925 vet nurses and 705 support staff (receptionists in clinics and head office staff).The ratio of veterinarians to vet nurses to support staff is broadly in line with industry averages. PVS has a financial year end of 31 March. Strategic position Although the prices charged by some other pet vet organisations are significantly lower than those charged by PVS,PVS has differentiated its service offering using three service features. These features have historically driven PVS's growth and provided competitive advantage. Service feature Location Over 85%of PVS clinics are in town centres.These locations have proved popular because of their convenience.They have car parking for those clients who prefer to drive and are easy to access on foot or using public transport. Innovation PVS has developed a wide range of innovative services in pet care, including therapy for pets experiencing behavioural problems and the use of acupuncture for pain relief.It has won numerous awards for these innovations. Quality of service PVS has historically had an excellent reputation for the quality of client service and patient care provided by knowledgeable,happy,and engaged staff from welcoming and comfortable clinics.It has prided itself on knowing its market and giving its clients what they want These features contributed to PVS winning Arland's Vet Organisation of the Year 11 times,the last time being six years ago. However,as the industry has consolidated and competition has increased,PVS's revenue has started to decline.Market share has dropped every year since 20X0. In the last couple of years,several competitors have been aggressively undercutting PVS's prices.Although they offer a very different service culture to PVS's,they have been taking market share. Ownership Pietr Praecuro retired recently.He no longer has any active involvement in the management of PVS,but he still owns 11·11%of the shares. The board of PVS has eight executive directors,including an executive chair and a recently appointed chief executive officer(CEO).Each of the executives own 11·11%of the shares.PVS is unlisted and is all equity-financed. Board and organisational structure As well as the eight executive directors there is one non-executive director. Executive chairChiefexecutiveofficerBusinessdevelopmentdirectorChief medical officerChief finance officerHuman resourcesdirectorChiefoperatingofficerLegal directorNED Executive chair Chief executive officer Business development director Chief medical officer Chief finance officer Human resources director Chief operating officer Legal director NED As a non-listed company,the directors made the decision not to have audit,nomination and remuneration committees.There is an internal audit function which reports to the chief finance officer. The chief operating officer (COO)has overall responsibility for the day-to-day operations of PVS's clinics.Individual clinics are managed by clinic managers.Up to ten clinic managers report to each of PVS's senior veterinarians,who in turn report to one of five district managers. The district managers report to the COO but also work with the other executives on,for example, HR or legal issues arising in the clinics for which they are responsible. DistrictmanagersClinicmanagersSenior District managers Clinic managers COo veterinarians The other board members'areas of responsibility are as follows: Chief finance officer(CFO) Finance,purchasing and internal audit functions Chief medical officer(CMO) Quality of patient care and medical treatment provided in PVS's clinics Human resources(HR) director Talent management,compensation and employee benefits,training (in conjunction with the CMO),health and safety Legal director Minimising PVS's legal risks by advising the other directors and senior management on any major legal and regulatory issues PVS may encounter Business development(BD) director Increasing PVS's revenue by identifying and developing new business opportunities,as well as expanding brand presence Most decision making is centralised,for example,on pricing,the roll out of new services and salary levels.District managers and senior veterinarians are responsible for generating growth for the clinics under their management within the parameters set by the board and for using resources efficiently and effectively. Information systems and use of technology The business management software used by PVS to record client and patient information and schedule consultations is an off-the-shelf package which has been used by PVS for eight years. The PVS website includes a search functionality which enables prospective clients to find clinics near them.Basic information about the services offered by each clinic is included,as well as telephone contact details for booking consultations.The website also details the executive team and provides some limited information on PVS,its mission and values. Although emails are used within PVS and with suppliers,there is no facility for clients to communicate with clinics via email and PVS has only a very limited social media presence. There is a staff intranet which includes news about what is happening internally within PVS and in the wider industry.There is information and resources for staff and an area for blogs. Despite its past history of innovation,PVS is yet to make any major investment in technological advances in pet care. Services The size of a clinic determines the services offered.PVS's larger clinics can carry out a wider range of diagnosis and surgical procedures than smaller ones.If a smaller clinic cannot carry out a procedure or test,the patient is referred to a larger PVS clinic in the same area. Although historically PVS developed and introduced an innovative range of pet care services, there has been very little innovation in recent years and in the past two years there have not been any new service offerings. PVS's clinics are open from 8.30am to 5.30 pm,Monday to Friday.Its veterinarians provide emergency cover outside these opening hours on a rotational basis. To attract and retain clients inan increasingly competitive market,PVS started the PVS Healthcare Plan two years ago.The Plan provides annual vaccinations and other routine treatments to pets for a monthly fee,payable by direct debit.Clients who join the Plan(known as members)can also pay for the cost of expensive treatments and procedures at a later date.Although this has an impact on PVS's cash flows,the retention rate of Plan members as clients is higher than that of clients who are not Plan members. PVS has to date relied on the visibility of its town centre locations and word-of-mouth recommendations to attract new customers.Its marketing activities have been limited to posters in clinics and advertisements in local newspapers. Suppliers PVS sources all medication and supplies needed for pet treatment from Arland Vet Supplies Ltd, the largest vet wholesaler in Arland.It has a long-established relationship with the supplier and, because purchasing is carried out centrally by PVS's head office based on information from individual clinics,PVS receives good bulk purchase discounts. Pet food and accessories are sourced from a number of pet food suppliers,including the Gloas Group,which owns a number of pet food brands,and purchased directly by individual clinics. Risks PVS maintains a risk register and its risk management programme is the responsibility of the internal audit function.The risks covered on the risk register are common to most pet vet organisations in Arland.An extract of the risk register is shown below. Answers Strategic Professional-Essentials,SBL Strategic Business Leader(SBL) September/December 2024 Sample Answers In the Strategic Professional Examinations it is not always possible to publish suggested answers which comprehensively cover all the valid points which candidates might make.Credit will be given to candidates for points not included in the suggested answers, but which,nevertheless,are relevant to the requirements.In adition,in this integrated case study examination candidates may re-introduce points made in other questions or parts of questions as long as these are made in the specific context of the requirements of the question being answered. The suggested answers presented below inevitably give much more detail than would be expected from most candidates under examination conditions,and include most of the obvious points evidenced from the case information. The answers are therefore intended to provide a structure of the approach required from candidates,and cover the range and depth of knowledge relating to each task which might be demonstrated by the most well prepared and able candidates.They are also intended to support revision and tuition for future examinations. 1 To:Chief finance officer From:Senior business analyst Date:xx September 20X4 Report Beago's service portfolio Introduction The fllowing report will assess the portfolio of services currently offered by Beago,to help determine the most appropriate portfolio management approach.A useful tool to undertake this analysis is the Boston Consulting Group(BCG)matrix,which can be used to analyse product lines or services using two key variables:relative market share and the market growth rate.By combining these two variables into a matrix,we can plot Beago's business services to determine where to allocate extra resources,where to extract cash to support other growing services and where to divest.The main purpose of this analysis is to help make investment decisions at a corporate level. BCG matrix applied to Beago Relative market share Market growthMarket growthLow HighLow HighHigh Low Market growth Market growth Low High Low High STAR Micro mobility PROBLEM CHILD/QUESTION MARK Business services Ride hailing CASH COw DOG Ride sharing (Tutorial note: Although it is dificult for candidates to draw diagrams in the exam we are including this here as an aid to learning) Within the BCG matrix,the horizontal axis represents Relative market share',which measures our competitiveness.It is measured by comparing our market share relative to our largest competitor.To have a 'high'relative market share,we should ideally have at least a similar or equivalent market share to our largest competitor.The assumption is that an increase in relative market share will result in an increase in the generation of cash,as we will benefit from economies of scale and thus gain cost advantages relative to our competitors. On the vertical axis,'Market growth’is used as a measure of market attractiveness.We should focus primarily on growing markets as these are likely to produce higher returns on investment in the long term.However,investment in growing markets will be needed to make growth possible,such as in marketing campaigns or assets to increase capacity. Question marks (or problem children) These are categorised in the BCG matrix as services operating with a low market share in a high growth market.Importantly,these services have the potential to gain market share and become 'Stars'(market leaders),if managed well.However,to make this happen will require significant cash investments. Considering Beago's portfolio,ride hailing would fall into the question mark category,as currently it is experiencing a high market growth rate of 11%driven by customer preference for non-owned transport options to help them to access work and leisure facilities. Also,urban populations are predicted to grow significantly in the coming years which should drive the demand for ride hailing. Therefore,market growth is high.In terms of relative market share,although our market share has grown significantly in recent years in Bealand,it is sill low compared to our largest competitor,Zenbi.Therefore,the ride hailing service has the potential to become a star quite quickly,but only with more focus and more investment in winning market share to equal or overtake Zenbi. There is sill huge potential for the ride hailing service,and as our primary service offering,we should focus on investing further in this area to turn this into a'Star'.Further product enhancements in terms of enhanced customer services and additional focused and targeted marketing and promotional campaigns should be considered to keep moving the ride hailing services in the direction of a 'Star'. We would also classify our 'business services'as a question mark service.As the marketing report suggests,there is significant potential for market growth in this service area,with businesses seeking to find alternatives to owning their own vehicle fleets and/or searching for ways to optimise transportation for customers and staff around Bealand's cities.Market growth is high at 15%. However,relative market share is low compared to the highest competitor in the market,Dpart.Although we are currently in a strong position as the second biggest business services provider in the market,the market is currently fragmented and it will take significant investment,in setting up a specific or dedicated business services fleet or in extensive business advertising to increase our market share.Dpart has a significant advantage as it is a dedicated business services transport provider and therefore our focus should be on winning customers from the other players in this market who are likely to have fewer resources and a lower market profile than ourselves and Dpart. Stars These are services with a high relative market share in a fast-growing industry.Stars are likely to generate large amounts of cash due to their high relative market share but also require large investment to fight competitors and maintain their growth rate Our micro mobility transport solutions are increasing rapidly in popularity,driven by the focus on sustainable transport options. Bicycles are proving increasingly popular with younger customers as a sustainable,convenient and cheap urban transport solution. The growth rate is high at 14%p.a.In terms of relative market share,our recent investment in micro mobility infrastructure has resulted in us being the market leader in Bealand and therefore this gives us a high relative market share.Therefore,this service aspect of our business is a 'Star'.The consideration with such services is that to maintain our position and to turn this service into a future cash cow we can use to fund future developments,will require sustained investment. Cash cows Eventually after years of operating in the industry,market growth might decline,and revenues stagnate.At this stage,our stars are likely to transform into 'Cash cows'.However,because they sil have a large relative market share in a mature market,profits and cash flows are expected to be high.Because of a lower growth rate,investment needed should also be low.Cash cows therefore typically generate cash in excess of the amount of cash needed to maintain this service sector. A concerning aspect of Beago's current service portfolio is that it currently has no cash cows which can generate excess cash to maintain the growth and development of the rest of our service portfolio.Although our stars are generating cash,they are also consuming high levels of cash to sustain them.Our question mark services (ride hailing and business services)have huge potential for growth but will require significant investment to maintain them at the required rate,considering the competitive landscape. Therefore,without a cash cow we run the risk of having insufficient cash to allow us to sustain the growth we need to exploit the growing business environment,which may put us at a competitive disadvantage. Dogs Products or services in a low growth or declining market with a small relative market share are considered to be 'Dogs'.These services often can break even (they neither create nor consume a large amount of cash)and are likely to generate just enough cash to maintain their market share.These services are often not so interesting or appealing for investors,since money could be better invested in other services with more potential,Dogs are often divested. Using the BCG matrix criteria,our ride sharing service is classified as a Dog.This is because the growth rate is low at 6%,largely driven by customers preferring the ride hailing services which provide a non-shared form of transport.Also,the relative market share is low as our nearest competitor,Delle,has significantly more market share,as it focuses specifically on ride sharing solutions. However,to divest from ride sharing may not be a viable option for Beago.Although it is not in a growing market and we are not the market leaders,nor are we likely to be,the ride sharing service is an important aspect of our overall business proposition.We were founded on the basis of being a ride sharing business and to eliminate this from our portfolio may send out a damaging message to our stakeholders that we have moved away from our original principles.We would need to consider this option carefull.Currently, the provision of ride sharing is not likely to be excessively costly and the service will sill bring in cash to the business.Therefore,it plays an overall role in supporting our business and therefore should be maintained. Conclusion Overall,we have a relatively healthy portfolio of popular and growing services in growing markets.However,we must consider how we ensure that our star and question mark products are supported to ensure that they go on to become future cash cows which can support our future service developments. 2 (a)To:Chief finance officer From:Senior business analyst Date:xx September 20X4 Report Value created by the food delivery proposal Introduction Traditionally,the meaning of value has been associated with the present value of expected future cash flows,and value creation has been understood as the change in that measure of value due to an organisation's financial performance.However,a wider understanding of value is increasingly being recognised,where value creation goes beyond financial capital,to incorporate two further aspects:first,the shared value a business creates which benefits its direct stakeholders;and second,the value it generates for society and the environment. Value creation therefore encompasses other forms of value which the organisation creates through the increase,decrease or transformation of its capitals.Integrated reporting(<IR>)uses six capitals as a basis for reporting value creation.These six capitals and how the proposed food delivery service will impact on them are discussed below. Financial capital This capital element of <IR>refers to the pool of funds which is available to us for use in the provision of the services we deliver.This capital is primarily the one most important to our investors,who will be interested in the impact of the food delivery service on the capital funds needed to finance the proposal and the potential funds generated from its operations. The traditional measure of organisational performance,this capital includes funds obtained through financing or generated by means of productivity. The food delivery proposal is estimated to require a long-term loan of $350 million,which would be included in the overall financial capital(liability)of the business.A long-term loan will finance the proposal but will also require a commitment to pay the loan sum and the interest payments.This will be a significant liability for the business and value is therefore reduced as far as investors are concerned.However,the food delivery proposal suggests that the revenue from the food delivery business will increase by 8%p.a.for the next five years and that the growth potential from food delivery will last significantly longer than this.Therefore,investors should recognise that there is significant opportunity for increased added value to the financial capital through growth in revenues and hopefully profits into the future if the proposal is successfully implemented by Beago. Manufactured capital Manufactured capital relates to the physical objects which are available to us for use in the delivery of our services,including buildings and infrastructure. For the food delivery service,we will be setting up a newly built state-of-the-art head office facility in Bealand's capital city to house the new business unit and the 300 staff we expect to employ to operate the divisional activities.It is presumed that a significant proportion of the $350 million loan needed to fund this proposal will be used to construct this facility.Therefore,this will be a significant addition to Beago's manufactured capital and will be an important element which supports this business unit's ability to create value for Beago in the long term. Intellectual capital This capital relates to the organisation's knowledge-based intangibles,including our intllectual property,such as,copyrights, software and licences.Intellectual capital should also include our own internal staff knowledge,systems,procedures and protocols as wellas our brand and reputation which we have developed in our existing transport services to date. The proposed food delivery service should create value through the development of our new Beago Bites app,which will be a newly developed software application which customers can use to access the food outlets and restaurants.This willcreate additional intllectual property for us,upon which we can build knowledge and enhance our business value.The proposal suggests that we can use this new app to analyse routes,driver and customer behaviour which should also help to enhance our organisational knowledge and build on our business value and intellectual capital. Human capital This capital refers to staff competencies,capabilities and experience,and the skills and know-how of our staf,and their commitment and motivation. The proposal suggests that an additional 300 staff would be required to manage the new business unit across several business functions.It will be important to ensure that all new staff employed in the food delivery business unit are effectively selected and trained and operate at the same ethical standard as the rest of the business.Otherwise,this could destroy value in our human capital. The proposal refers to the opportunities presented for drivers and specifically for delivery drivers who want to operate more sustainably through the use,free of charge,of our micro mobility transport options.Therefore,we could attract a large number of additional drivers who wish to deliver food for us,adding to our human capital and at the same time reducing our environmental impact. We may also consider that this proposal may lose value for us in terms of its impact on the other parts of our business.If our current drivers move towards the food delivery service,it may impact detrimentally on our ride hailing operations and driver availability,so we need to look to ensure that our human capital is sufficient to operate our existing and proposed services effectively. Social and relationship capital This capital refers to relationships we have within and between communities,government and customers to enhance individual and collective well-being.It also includes the shared norms and common values we follow to build stakeholder relationships and trust. The proposal suggests that it aims to bring communities together through facilitating the connection of restaurants and food outlets with customers.Therefore,we are assisting in building relationships between stakeholders within the communities we serve.In addition,we are building relationships with new stakeholders -the food outlets. It could be argued that we are reducing social value by reducing the socialisation of individuals within and around the local communities.By encouraging people to eat at home,we could be accused of damaging or reducing social interactions within communities which could in fact harm the overall business communities where we operate and thus negatively impacting on value. Natural capital This capital includes renewable and non-renewable environmental resources such as water,fossil fuels,solar energy and land which support our organisation.Obviously,our new state of the art facility will consume significant natural capital in its construction and subsequent operation,therefore we need to ensure that as part of its state-of-the-art design,it includes thee latest environmental and sustainable design features,including ensuring it is built on a previously developed site and that it uses the most efficient energy consumption/generation technologies such as solar energy.One aspect of the food delivery proposal which could add value in this capital is the encouragement of delivery through our micro mobility transport options which should minimise car usage and therefore reduce harmful carbon emissions. (b)Appendix 1-Key performance indicators(KPls) 1.Percentage of on-time deliveries per day/week/month As a food delivery service,a critical measure of success in the eyes of our customers would be ensuring that the food is delivered on time.Late delivery of a product such as food could impact on customer enjoyment and quality of thee experience.We can set a time for each delivery based on expected miles between where the order is placed and thee customer's location,using our current app technology.We can then measure actual time taken to deliver each food order and monitor the percentage of on-time versus late deliveries.We could use this to evaluate whether this is due to customer location or potentially an issue with route planning on the app. 2.Number of new customers per month joining the food delivery app The success of this new service will be determined by how many customers we can attract.However,it may be dificult to set a target of expected customers per month initially,as we have no prior experience of this service.Therefore,it may be appropriate to measure growth in new customers over the first three to six months.This should give us a sound indication of customer interest in this service and the success of our initial marketing efforts. 3.Number of deliveries made by micro mobility driving options as %of total deliveries The food delivery service proposal is an opportunity for Beago to increase our positive impact on driving sustainable transport solutions.The proposal sets out the aim to encourage drivers to use micro mobility options to deliver the food orders,which would have a positive impact on reducing emissions from this new service.Therefore,a useful measure of performance in terms of goals towards sustainability would be the percentage of deliveries made by bicycle.We should be looking to increase this over time by motivating drivers to switch to micro mobility driving options. 4.Repeat orders A measure of satisfaction with the service we offer would be the number of repeat orders from customers.We would need to monitor the number of customers we have and measure how many of these customers order multiple times within a given period.If we have a low percentage of customers who are ordering more than once in a month,then we need to consider the effectiveness of the service offered or how we achieve more repeat business.This could be by offering promotional discounts for increased activity.High repeat orders would be an indication that the food delivery service is effective and valued by the customers and that we are successful in retaining their business. Other KPls could include: - Customer satisfaction rates per month Driver/customer churn rate per month - Number of new restaurant chains/food outlets added per month Number of food orders placed per month 3 (a)(i) Slide 1 Benefits of cloud computing technology Cost - Scalability Business continuity 一 Performance and access Notes Cost Cloud computing eliminates the capital expense of buying hardware and software and setting up and running our own on-site data management centres.These costs are likely to be significant and would include the servers,the 24-hour cost of power and cooling and the IT professionals needed to manage such infrastructure,which would allbe incurred by thee cloud service provider. Scalability Using the facilities offered by cloud service providersallows us to upor down scale as we require,depending on our needs at any particular time.As our business continues to grow,cloud computing allows us to do this technologically,without having to invest in significant hardware and software upgrades ourselves.This provides us with a high degree of flexibility. Business continuity Cloud computing ensures continuous back-up of our data.In the event of an attack or a crisis event,downtime will therefore be minimised,and we can continue our operations with minimum disruption to customers.This is essential in our business operations.The information held in the cloud can be recovered very quickly if the system is compromised by an attack or other emergency. Up-to-date performance an access Cloud computing services run on a worldwide network of secure data centres,which are regularly upgraded to the latest generation of fast and efficient computing hardware.This offers several benefits including greater economies of scale which helps to keep costs down and ensuring that our business is using the most up-to-date technologies available without us having to invest ourselves in such expensive upgrades.The fact the data is all off-site means that problems like an issue with local hardware,for example,a power cut or malware attack,does not create a permanent loss of data. Cloud data will remain safe and can still be accessed by any device connected to the internet. Slide 2 Risks of cloud computing technology Greater attack risk Accountability Service level agreements Reduces our own information systems expertise Notes Greater attack risk As we can see from the recent article,the risks to cloud computing service providers is increasing.Cloud computing data centres are likely to be environments where there are huge amounts of computing power,data and operators which creates a bigger target for hackers and cyber criminals.The rewards for a successful attack on cloud providers is potentially huge for criminals and therefore makes cloud service providers an ever-increasing target. Accountability Moving services to the cloud transfers some of the risk management duties to the cloud service provider,but it is only thee management of the risks which is transferred.Accountability for the actual risks still resides with us.Our operational risk management framework must therefore take account of the special situations arising from cloud service adoption. Service level agreements(SLAs) The responsibilities of the cloud service provider and our own responsibilities are likely to be complex and therefore drawing up and then managing the SLA is likely to be challenging.As the cloud service provider is likely to have an advantage in terms of knowledge and experience in managing multiple businesses,we may be tied into a long-term contract which is not entirely suitable for our own unique information system needs. Reduction on own information systems expertise Although we are a transport business,our operations are largely driven by technology.Although we have retained our own app development and research and design teams,the handing over of our hardware and software management to a third party means we rely on them instead of our internal experts.This can become a risk if our competitors are able to gain competitive advantage by retaining their own expertise in-house and thus develop greater business experience than ourselves. (b)From:Senior business analyst <SBA@Beago.co.bl> To:Chief finance officer <CFO@Beago.co.bl> Date:xx September 20X4 Subject:RE:Disruptive technologies Good day Trends in disruptive technologies and applications indicate that the nature of both transportation and work is changing. Disruptive technologies may also change how the Bealand government anticipates and meets citizen expectations for mobility and how it subsequently invests in transportation infrastructure.However,we could see these as opportunities as well as potential threats to Beago. Driverless(autonomous)vehicles The application of driverless vehicles in transportation networks would seem inevitable as the technology develops and is further refined by other TNCs.However,it does still seem some way away before fully autonomous vehicles will be allowed to operate in our cities,as most development is currently only at the testing stage.Certainly,there are risks involved,both operationally and reputationally. The CEO is correct in being concerned about the costs of autonomous vehicle technology but if we wait until the technology is further enhanced and refined,we will not have to spend the development costs which some other businesses are incurring. Being the first mover is not always an advantage and we can learn from the mistakes of others.The risks of reputational damage,should accidents occur with autonomous technology,are indeed a concern but not significantly more that potential reputational damage from accidents which occur with driver enabled vehicles.The risks are always present and,in fact, driverless technology in theory should eliminate many of the risks associated with human drivers such as excessive speed, driver distractions and driving too close to other vehicles.In theory,driverless cars should in fact be safer and more cost effective in the long term. Internet of Things (loT) The first example of loT application affecting Beago mentioned by the CEO was local authorities using sensors in traffic signals to remotely and instantly change traffic signals to direct and control traffic within the busiest and most congested city areas. The CEO believes that this could also mean our own route-planning systems used to work out optimum routes for customers could be disrupted by these systems,adding more miles to customer journeys and therefore cost to customers.This could be true,but we should see this as an opportunity to work collaboratively with local authorities to develop these systems.We are a technology driven business and we could offer our expertise to local authorities to develop route planning technologies which are built around the loT traffic control sensors.Our on-board trafic and route data could be exploited as part of the system to build the clear picture of traffic within city centres.Obviously,we would need to review our pricing policies to ensure that customers are not disadvantaged by route changes.Another advantage for us of being involved in this system is that it fits with our focus on environmental sustainability,so if we are part of the solution in reducing city centre congestion and also air pollution,then this may impact positively on our reputation. A second loT application is the introduction of cameras,which read car licence plates in designated clear air zones(CAZ). Only electric vehicles(EVs)will be exempt from charges.The CEO is particularly concerned that this will impact on our drivers who may be discouraged from driving for us within city centres as they will be charged each time they enter a designated CAZ, which could be a significant number of times daily.This is a genuine concern and is likely to discourage drivers from accepting rides which have been requested within a CAZ.If we were to charge customers higher rates for journeys within these zones to cover driver charges,this would likely result in a loss of customers,so this is unlikely to be an option.Compensating drivers for travelling in these zones could be an option but is likely to be expensive for us. However,it could be an opportunity for us to exploit our micro mobility transport solutions to customers in CAZs.It may also be an opportunity for us to encourage drivers to switch to EV use or encourage drivers with EV cars to drive for us.If we switched to more EV driven vehicles,then this would not only overcome the CAZ charges but would also enhance our environmental sustainability credentials. I hope that this analysis has assisted.
