Corjetz headline results and cost breakdown 1 1.Introduction Corjetz is a low-cost regional airline business.It was established 20 years ago in Corland,a country located in the continent of Hundria.Over the years,it has positioned itself as one of the world's leading low-cost airlines and is currently the second largest low-cost airline business operating in Hundria (in terms of passenger numbers). Corjetz currently operates nearly 1,000 routes (destinations)in over 30 countries across Hundria. Its head office is based at Near City airport,located in the south of Corland.Hundria is apolitically stable and economically developed continent of the world.Most of the countries in Hundria form a trading bloc which facilitates free trade and free movement of people within it.Each country in Hundria uses its own currency.Corland's currency is the $. Corjetz operates a 'point-to-point'service,meaning that it operates its flights directly between destinations.This means that its passengers do not have to travel to or change flights at a 'hub' airport to reach their destination,which helps Corjetz to reduce its operating costs.It also keeps costs low through its 'no-frills'approach to flight delivery.This approach means that in-flight meals are not provided (unless paid for separately)and includes the automation of a wide range of its processes,including bookings,flight documentation,baggage drop and flight check-in. Additionally,it operates a profitable and popular portfolio of routes between over 130 airports across Hundria. 2.Airline industry information Overview The airline industry encompasses a wide range of businesses called airlines which offer an air transport service to customers.Normally referred to as commercial airlines,these businesses offer scheduled commercial flights which carry both human passengers and cargo, and in most cases are carried out using jet aircrafts.Commercial flights are those which transport passengers from one destination to another with a pre-booked ticket.Non-commercial flights are either operated privately or by the military. The airline industry forms just one sector of the wider aviation industry, which also includes all other aviation-related businesses,such as jet engine suppliers,aircraft manufacturers and aerospace companies. The airline industry directly and indirectly supports global trade,tourism,and economic growth. Therefore,it has had a major influence on globalisation in many other industries. Passenger types A leisure passenger is someone travelling on holiday to stay in a hotel or a holiday resort to relax and/or experience local tourist attractions.A business passenger is someone travelling for work purposes and will normally be attending a business meeting or conference.Both types of passengers may be offered the option to travel first class,business class or economy class by some airlines. First class passengers pay significantly more for their ticket but in return receive a high-quality service,including dedicated airport lounges,large and comfortable in-flight seats and unlimited food and drink provided throughout the flight.Business class passengers pay less than first class passengers for their tickets and receive a slightly lower level of service.Economy class passengers pay even less for their flight ticket than business class passengers.An economy price ticket normally includes a standard seat with limited leg room and standard and limited in-flight food options. Airline types-by service International airlines International airlines are the largest and often the most well-known airlines. International airlines offer global services,carrying leisure and business passengers and cargo over long distances.International airlines usually have multiple airport hubs which provide customers with access to hundreds of global destinations. National airlines National airlines typically focus on offering services within their home country,but many also offeraccess to international destinations.A national airline is likely to have a smaller aircraft fleet than the international airlines and often also offer services to both leisure and business passengers. Regional airlines Regional airlines tend to focus on offering services within specific regions, for example,a continent.In some cases,they provide passenger services to locations not offered by either national or international airlines. Airline types-by business model Full-service carriers Full-service carriers offer a range of pre-flight and on-board services within the price of the ticket.This may include checked-in baggage, in-flight meals,airport lounges and multiple service classes,such as first class,business class and economy class.They usually fly national, international,long-haul,and short-haul flights. Low-cost carriers Low-cost carriers aim to gain a competitive advantage over full-service carriers by reducing costs.This is done in several ways,including having smaller fleets and smaller aircraft,by offering a more limited service and by flying to smaller and less popular airports,where landing and ground handling fees are cheaper. Forcustomers,flights from low-cost carriers are often significantly cheaper. Services such as in-flight meals and baggage allowances will normally not be included in the price of the ticket.Instead,where these facilities are available,customers pay extra for them.Some low-cost carriers offer both economy and business class services. Charter airlines Charter airlines focus on providing passenger services to tourists to a specific destination.In most cases,they do not sell airline tickets directly but instead enter into agreements with tour operators who then take responsibility for finding passengers for them. Cargo airlines The main focus of cargo airlines is the transportation of cargo or freight. Some cargo airlines are subsidiaries or sub-divisions of airlines which also offer passenger services. Hub and spoke versus point-to-point travel Airlines which operate a hub and spoke model fly from one airport into a hub airport,with passengers then connecting from the hub onto a flight to their final destination.Airlines operate this model because it allows them to offer more flights to passengers and is designed to optimise an airline's network coverage.The alternative to this is the point-to-point model,where the airline focuses on providing passengers with a route from origin to final destination.In this model,there is no need to connect passengers through a hub airport,as flights operate directly between cities.This model has proved popular with airline customers in recent years and most of the low-cost airlines operate this model. POINT-TO-POINT POINT-TO-POINT Key air transport statistics 20X2 Key statistics People transported (passengers per year) Average number of commercial flights per day Passenger revenues HUB-AND-SPOKE HUB-AND-SPOKE 20X2 Global 3 billion 73,000 $390 billion $79·2 billion20X2 Hundria $79·2 billion 15,000600 million 15,000 Flight management activities Flight management activities are provided by airports.Airlines pay for these services via airport fees. Air traffic control (ATC) Air traffic control (ATC)is a vital aspect of air transportation.It is carried out by ground-based personnel specially trained in monitoring air traffic in a specific location.ATC is responsible for moving aircraft safely and efficiently through the airspace system.Air traffic controllers ensure that aircraft remain at set distances apart whilst moving them between airports along set flight paths. Aircraft ground handling Ground handling refers to the wide range of services provided to facilitate passengers within an airport and to facilitate an aircraft's ground positioning both before and after a flight.Ground handling services include: - Baggage management-including sorting passenger baggage and loading and unloading baggage onto and off aircraft. - Customer service management -including gate assignment,and pre-flight facilities management (such as operating shops and waiting areas in airports). Aircraft boarding management -including operating shuttle bus services between the airport and the aircraft,ramp facilities and passenger travel documentation management. Aircraft towing and re-fuelling services -including movement of an aircraft to or from a parking stand or from one gate to another and the re-fuelling of aircrafts using fuel purchased by the airline. Airport security-including pre-flight passenger screening and security checks,baggage scanning,passport control and all on-site security management activities carried out to protect airport buildings,runways,and machinery. Industry challenges Demand is cyclical,with demand for flights driven by economic growth.In the last ten years, low-cost carriers have increasingly taken market share from full-service carriers by offering passengers affordable and low-cost air travel.This has made air travel more accessible to a much wider customer base globally.It has also introduced a high level of competition to the industry. The airline industry has been impacted by several geopolitical events in recent years,such as terror attacks,cyber-attacks and extreme weather events caused by climate change.These have led to both short and long-term consequences for demand.Terror and cyber-attacks have impacted on customer confidence in the industry and climate change is increasingly prominent on the global political agenda. Fuel is one of the biggest costs for airlines,and one of the most volatile.Fuel represents over 20% of most airlines'cost base annually.The price of jet fuel is strongly correlated to the international price of crude oil which has been highly volatile in the last five years.Fuel costs are particularly prone to foreign exchange movements,which can also have a significant impact on this major cost. The airline industry is still some way away from operating aircraft with alternative fuels such as biofuels,and most airlines are still fully reliant on crude oil.Governments and customers are becoming increasingly environmentally sensitive and the expectation for airlines to operate in a 'greener'way is increasing.If the airline industry is to survive in the long term,it will need to invest significantly in alternative fuel sources. Environmental factors are a major and increasingly significant issue for the airline industry, affecting both passenger demand and operating parameters.Customers are increasingly aware that their carbon footprint from airline travel is contributing around 3%of global carbon emissions. Therefore,many are actively considering alternatives to air travel. Despite environmental concerns,commercial international air traffic(aircraft operating in the skies)increased by 5%in 20X2.According to the latest industry data,the international air traffic control environment is struggling to cope with increasing flight numbers,evidenced by the number of delayed flights rising by nearly 20%in 20X2.Similarly,airports around the world are struggling to cope with demand from airlines for flight slots1.This is leading to increased congestion at airports,resulting in more delays for passengers.This is also driving up aircraft ground handling service charges for all airlines. 1Aflight slot is the authorisation to either land or take off at a particular airport on a particular day during a specified time period.Flight slots are paid for by the airlines. Industry key performance indicators(KPls) Airlines use multiple performance metrics to measure and monitor their performance. Some examples are provided in the table below: KPI Definition 1.Available seat kilometres(ASK) ASK is calculated by taking the number of seats available and multiplying by the distance flown.It is a measure of airline capacity. 2.Revenue per available seat kilometre(RASK) RASK is calculated by dividing the airline's total revenue by its total ASK.It is a measure of potential revenue generation. 3.Load factor Calculated as the percentage of checked-in passengers in relation to an aircraft's available seats.It is a measure of utilisation. 4.Revenue passenger kilometres(RPK) RPK is calculated as the number of passengers multiplied by the total distance travelled.It shows the number of kilometres travelled by paying passengers who are referred to as 'revenue passengers'. 5.CO₂per passenger (grams) Calculated as total CO₂produced by the airline divided by the number of passengers. 6.Punctuality The percentage of flights which land on time at the destination airport.Typically,this means landing within 15 minutes of the scheduled arrival time. Industry body The airline industry has a global trade association.Its role is to help formulate industry policy and support airline activity.It promotes awareness of the benefits which the airline industry brings to national and global economies.Its role is also to develop global standards for the air transport industry,and it provides advice on airline safety and security by setting clearly defined rules. 3.Corjetz overview Background Corjetz was established in Corland 20 years ago by entrepreneurial brothers Eric and Axel Madson.They both had worked for many years in the airline industry and had a keen interest in travelling.With money inherited from their parents,they acquired two second-hand aircraft.They started by operating flights from Near City airport,located 40 miles from Corland's capital city in the south of the country,to airports located close to two cities in the north of the country. Using their knowledge and experience of the airline industry,Eric and Axel spotted a gap in the market in Corland for more convenient and affordable low-cost air travel.Up to that point, Corland's airline industry was dominated by full-service carriers. The low-cost business model in the Corland airline industry proved extremely popular with air passengers,who liked the convenience of Corjetz's point-to-point service and the significantly cheaper ticket prices than those offered by existing full-service carriers. Over the years,Corjetz has focused on operating flights to and from airports across Hundria with the highest customer demand and continually looks for opportunities to extend its network.Cost efficiency has been its overriding strategic focus.This has been delivered through developing and maintaining long-term strategic partnerships with aircraft manufacturers,key airports,ground handling operators and maintenance contractors who maintain Corjetz's owned aircrafts,and by operating the latest and most efficient aircraft. Over the last 20 years the low-costmodel of air travel has continuedBrygair(Broland)continent.Corjetz(Corland)14%LKK(Lakland)to be very popular with customersacross Hundria,and there are now 30Low cost airline market shareHundria 20X2(passenger numbers)low-cost airlines operating across theOthers(27 airlines)60%17%9% Over the last 20 years the low-cost model of air travel has continued Brygair (Broland) continent. Corjetz (Corland) 14% LKK (Lakland) to be very popular with customers across Hundria,and there are now 30 Low cost airline market share Hundria 20X2(passenger numbers) low-cost airlines operating across the Others (27 airlines) 60% 17% 9% Eric and Axel both retired from the business 10 years ago but since their departure,Corjetz has remained focused on maintaining and strengthening its low-cost strategic position. Corjetz growth 1100 300 200 100 Routes(Destinations) 10 YEARSIN OPERATION 20 Ownership Corjetz was listed on the Corland stock market nine years ago,one year after Eric and Axel retired.The brothers originally retained a 35%share in the business when it was first listed but since then,they have sold most of their shareholdings in Corjetz and they no longer play any role in running the business.Currently,80%of Corjetz's shares are held by large institutional investors,10%of shares are held by Corjetz employees and the remaining shares are held by small private investors.Magda Fisk is the current CEO of Corjetz and has been in place for 10 years.She was instrumental in achieving Corjetz's listing. Corjetz key resources Routes Corjetz has a profitable and extensive portfolio of routes which it operates to and from over 130 airports across Hundria.This makes it very popular with customers,who find the range of destinations and frequency of flights offered by Corjetz very convenient and attractive.It regularly reviews its route network to exploit the latest demand opportunities. Fleet Corjetz operates a modern fleet of 300 aircraft,of which 75%are owned outright and the remaining 25%are leased (leasing is a common form of financing in the airline industry).Corjetz has invested significantly in the latest aircraft which are more fuel efficient and environmentally friendly.This has contributed significantly to its low-cost strategic position and has also positively impacted on reducing carbon emissions.Corjetz's average aircraft is 4·5 years old,which is low compared to the industry standard. Corjetz focuses on leisure passengers rather than on business passengers,therefore its aircraft cabins consist of economy class seats only with a high-density layout (this gives Corjetz 150 seats per aircraft compared to 140 seats on similar sized aircraft operated by its competitors). This higher seat density allows Corjetz to offer lower ticket prices per flight which is attractive to customers. Technology Corjetz applies data analytics extensively throughout its business and considers data to be a key business resource.It has always been an early adopter of the latest technologies in the airline industry.It currently makes extensive use of technology throughout its business operations, including: - A mobile app which allows passengers to book flights,check in for flights,download boarding passes (to either print off or store as a mobile boarding pass on the passenger's smartphone)and receive real-time flight status information.The app significantly reduces the cost of issuing,distributing and processing physical tickets.An enhanced feature of the mobile app is the integration of live data from the airport's information systems with the passenger's booking and flight details,allowing personalised instructions and updates to be sent to passengers before their journey. In most airports Corjetz operates fully automated baggage check-in machines to help reduce check-in queues. All aircraft are fitted with electronic touchpads for the crew,to replace laptops and printed navigational charts for pilots.This has replaced the need for each flight to carry heavy printed flight logbooks which has helped to reduce CO₂emissions per flight. AIl Corjetz's aircraft are fitted with big data servers to collect continuous in-flight data about flight and engine performance. Corjetz continually searches fornew technologies toenhanceandsupport its business operations, to improve efficiency and reduce costs. People Corjetz employs nearly 11,800 staff,including over 2,000 pilots and 6,500 cabin crew.The remaining staff fulfila number of roles,including staff operating at airports and head office staff operating in IT,finance,customer services and human resource management.Regular updates are provided to employees on key business issues which include a regularly updated intranet, a monthly 'meet the board'online chat,and a weekly staff newsletter.All Corjetz employees undergo a full induction programme and have access to online learning resources,as well as career development opportunities.All employees receive regular and scheduled managerial appraisal of their performance. Board structure Corjetz has six executive directors and five NEDs(including the chair).The board structure is as follows: Non-executive chairSeniorNon-executive directordirectorChief people officerNon-executive Non-executiveChiefcommercialofficerChiefinformationofficerdirector directorChiefmarketingofficerChief finance officerChiefexecutive officerindependentnon-executive Non-executive chair Senior Non-executive director director Chief people officer Non-executive Non-executive Chief commercial officer Chief information officer director director Chief marketing officer Chief finance officer Chief executive officer independent non-executive There are four sub-committees: Nomination committee一 Audit committee Nomination committee Remuneration committee Remuneration committee 一 Safety committee Risk management and key risks Corjetz has a detailed risk register,and a robust enterprise risk management (ERM)framework is in place.The board takes responsibility for determining the nature and extent of the risks it is willing to take to achieve its strategic objectives.It is also responsible for maintaining and monitoring the systems of internal control and risk management.Risk management activities are co-ordinated by the risk and assurance team,led by a risk manager who reports to both the chief financial officer,and to the chair of the audit committee.Corjetz's key risks include: Safety and security of customers and staff.Delivering safe and secure operations which meet the needs and expectations of customers and staff is critical to the business. The growing impact of climate change and increasing expectations from customers, governments,regulators,and wider society for a greater focus on the environment and sustainability activities. Macro-economic and geopolitical events such as general economic trends,foreign exchange rates and volatile fuel prices which all impact financial performance. The availability,security and performance of its website and its critical business operating systems. 4.Corjetz website extracts What we do What we do We consistently deliver low fares to our customers and operate flights to the places our customers most want to travel to,using our cost advantage and leading position at airports across Hundria Mission statementTo connect the people of Hundria,through affordable and sustainable air travel. Q1-A Suggested Solution Requirement 1(a) Slide 1 Key entrepreneurial qualities of Corjetz's founders 1.Risk taking 2.Innovative 3.Visionary Leadership 4.Strong Product knowledge Key entrepreneurial qualities of Corjetz's founders Ability to take a risk Starting a new venture such as a new airline involved a huge amount of risk of failure.The founders could not be certain that the low-cost carrier approach would be taken up by customers, therefore the risks were high. Innovation The founders had a completely new approach to air travel in a very well-established market.Their low-cost proposition achieved the same thing as the traditional carriers but in a more efficient and economical way. Visionary leadership To be successful,entrepreneurs should have a clear vision of their new venture.The founders of Corjetz had a vision of the direction of the airline industry today.They were inspirational leaders who guided employees towards this direction. Strong product knowledge The owners knew the air transport market very well and were knowledgeable of the latest trends in the market.It is essential for them to know whether the new low-cost service would meet the demands of the market and to know when it is time for change. (ii)Slide 2 Leadership style shaping culture of innovation ·Culture of innovation ·Freedom to make decisions and make mistakes · Inclusion-make use of operational knowledge ·Celebrate success/history-stories ·Team building and brainstorming ·Control and risk management focus Corjetz culture Corjetz has a culture of innovation,which was established from the very start,as it was the first low-cost airline operator in Corland.This drive for innovation is assisted by its adoption of innovative technologies and through its overall low-cost business strategy. Leadership style and culture The leaders of Corjetz try to encourage the innovation culture by giving staff the freedom to make their own decisions and to make mistakes.Innovation is unlikely if staff feel constrained or afraid to challenge the norm to find new ways of operating. The leaders also encourage leadership and participation in decision making.Staff are the ones working on the front line and will have the most experience to challenge current ways of operating and find new ways.By encouraging participation,they are more likely to put forward ideas for innovation.The leaders recognise and value the expertise of the staff they employ,which will encourage staff to come forward with innovative ideas and opportunities. Corjetz's leaders also celebrate and share successes in the business.This is an important part of the cultural web,as shared successes will encourage staff to want to feel part of that success and therefore find ways to innovate,Team-building and brainstorming events are key routines in the business that also help to encourage a culture of innovation. Also sharing the past history of the business is an important way of focusing staff on what Corjetz does and how it has got its position and to celebrate that journey of innovation.Stories are an important part of encouraging buy-in to the culture. Leadership expects full commitment to the strong focus on control and risk management and that the business operates with the highest standards of safety.Therefore,there is stilla strong leadership focus on control and risk management,but this is done within the context of the innovative way in which Corjetz operates. Contributing towards the communities where our customers live and work makes good business sense and enables us to operate sustainably. Attract/retain employees Socially responsible businesses attract employees who are eager to make a difference in the world.Employees enjoy working more for organisations that have a positive public image than one that doesn't.By showing that you're committed to things like human rights,you're much more likely to attract and retain the top candidates.The workplace willbe more positive and productive, and by encouraging staff to work in the community,we are promoting personal and professional growth. An advantage over competitors By embracing CSR,we can try to stand out from our competitors in the airline industry. Establishing ourselves as a company committed to going one step further than other airline operators in considering our social and environmental impacts in how we operate,sets us apart. However,we must actively deliver on these differences and not just pay lip service to them in order to maintain this competitive edge. Q2-A Requirement 2(a) To: Chief executive officer From: Senior business analyst Date; September 20X3 Subject:Evaluation of strategic proposals and financing options Introduction The following report willevaluate the strategic development proposals outlined by the executive directors of Corjetz.The report willalso consider the possible sources of finance and recommend the most appropriate development proposal and source of finance for Corjetz. Strategic proposals The following proposals will be evaluated below: 1.Route expansion in Hundria 2.Offer business class seats on flights Proposal 1:Route expansion in Hundria As we will be operating exclusively within the Hundrian market and offering the same low-cost flights to customers (i.e.,the same product),then this option would be a market penetration strategy.Market penetration is likely to be the least risky growth strategy to adopt,as clearly,we have vast experience in the Hundrian market,and we are merely proposing to offer customers 'more of the same'.There is evidence of demand for more flights within Hundria and there are more areas of this market that Corjetz could exploit.Therefore,it is suitable in terms of our current strategic position.However there a couple of points that we would need to consider,relating to this strategy. Firstly,we would need to undertake more market research into the demand for flights to the cities where we do not currently fly to.These may not be viable destinations for large numbers of our customers and therefore flights offered my not be full and therefore not financially viable. Secondly,although research suggests that there is a high demand for flights to destinations where we only fly for a limited number of months,there is also the issue of airport capacity.Increasing the number of flights may not be achievable in some of these locations if airport capacity is already under pressure.Also,more flights into busier airports are likely to mean potential delays for passengers and a lower quality of on-ground service,which impacts on our own reputation.We would have to undertake extensive research and consultation with airports to make sure that quality of service could still be achieved for our customers.We would also have to consider the additional costs of airport fees and marketing costs to increase customer awareness of our expanded flights. Proposal 2:Offer business class seats on flights The CCO has proposed that Corjetz should offer a business class service to existing destinations. This is an example of a product development strategy,as we are offering a new product(business class service)to our existing destinations within Hundria. Market research suggests that there is a growing demand for business class service on flights in Hundria and as we have huge experience in this location,then expanding our offerings to include business class travel would seem a sensible development opportunity.In addition,business class passengers would pay double the price for a seat compared to economy class passengers,but this would of course mean that the quality and level of service offered would have to be increased. Aircraft would need to be re-configured to accommodate both business and economy class passengers and there would have to be a discernible difference between the services that we offer.As we have no experience of delivering business class services,significant research would be needed to ascertain their specific needs to ensure that we could differentiate our business class delivery from current,more experienced,competitors. As we are late to the business class travel market,and we are not known for this type of service delivery,it may take significant marketing campaigns to convince current passengers that we can deliver such services to the same standard as other business class service operators.Current operators are likely to react aggressively to such a move and are likely to put forward their own aggressive marketing campaigns to retain their customers.This could be damaging to us and highly expensive to present similar marketing initiatives.We would have to weigh up the costs and benefits of such a campaign. One of the biggest advantages is that we can charge a premium for business class tickets. However,for this price we must deliver to customer expectations,as should we fail,it could damage our reputation. Recommendation Both proposals have merits and definite potential for positive growth for Corjetz.However,we need to weigh up these benefits against the potential costs and timeframes for delivery.Both proposals would seem viable for growth within the given timescales,but we would need significant research specifically in the business class service delivery proposal before acquiring/leasing aircraft configured to deliver this service.Proposal 1 would probably seem to be the least risky option at this time and one which could still deliver growth in our target market. Financing The first financing option alternative is to take on more debt.As a business we are currently highly geared,but this is normal in the airline industry,and we are currently below the industry average. It is unlikely that the additional 25 aircraft(which is approximately an additional 8%to our current fleet of aircraft)will make a significant impact on our current gearing level.However,we have strict debt covenants on our existing debt,and we will need to carefully assess whether more debt is viable.However,we have a sound relationship with current debt providers and high debt is a common feature of the airline industry and therefore obtaining more debt may be a viable option. The second option is to lease the 25 aircraft.We currently lease 25%of our fleet which is a total of 75 aircraft.This arrangement would increase our leased percentage of aircraft to 32.5%of total aircraft(100 out of 325 aircraft).Leasing is a common arrangement in the airline industry,and we are familiar with this financing arrangement with our current lease partner.The aircraft willbe available in the desired timeline and are similar to the aircraft we already operate.Should we choose to deliver proposal 2 we will need to ensure that our lease partner can readily convert these aircraft to deliver both business and economy class travel facilities.However,this would appear to be a readily available and viable financing option Recommendation Based on the above analysis and considering our current financing position in regard to other aircraft,I would suggest opting for a leasing arrangement with our current lease partner.This option would enable us to maintain our healthy liquidity position and would also ensure our gearing remains stable.We have established relationships with our current lease partners and therefore this option is likely to cause least disruption and will be straightforward to arrange as we already have established relationships with the lease partner. Requirement 2b To: Chair of the Audit Committee <Auditcommitteechair@Corjetz.com> From: Senior business analyst<SBA@Corjetz.com> Date: September 20X3 Subject:Risk Assessment Update Dear Chair l am responding on behalf of the chief executive officer who has asked me to present you with an explanation of the information contained in the recent email from the risk manager. Firstly,let me briefly explain the framework currently used by the Risk and Assurance team to assess the organisations risks.This matrix is known as the TARA(or sometimes SARA) framework. The TARA framework This is a tool which can help us assess and manage risks.The framework defines four scenarios based on whether the likelihood and the impact of a risk is high or low.The latest risk assessment carried out by the Risk and Assurance team identified four current risks which they considered to be increasing.We can use the TARA framework to assist in identifying ways to manage these risks effectively. Cyber risks -Transfer Threats from cyber-attacks on our critical operating systems are an ever-present and increasing threat to the business.The impact/consequence of such a threat could be catastrophic, particularly if our in-flight systems were breached,threatening the life of our passengers and staff. Similarly,any downtime in our key operating systems could lead to serious delays or loss of data which could impact on our business operations and ultimately our reputation. Although the probability of these threats is increasing,largely due to the sophistication of hacking techniques and because of the reliance we continue to place on the effective operation of our systems,the likelihood is still relatively low.This is because we are able manage this risk through employing expert third party service providers for many of our critical IT systems.Through these third-party experts we can share or transfer the risk to those who have more expertise than ourselves in monitoring and managing this risk.The expertise offered by these third-party suppliers should allow us to reduce the likelihood of this risk. Financial risks -Reduce Fuel currently accounts for over 20%of Corjetz's costs and therefore any significant fluctuation in this cost will impact on our bottom line.This is an issue for us because our shareholders are likely to be expecting steady growth and performance.Although short term volatility is expected and can be useful to make short term gains,in the long term,shareholders will not look favourably on our business if earnings are volatile.The risk assessment report indicates that political instability in the oil producing regions in the last three months has caused significant fluctuations in crude oil prices which would suggest that the likelihood of rising fuel prices willbe high. In terms of impact,it willcertainly impact on our profitability,but it willnot mean that the business operations will be disrupted and there should be no consequences for passengers in the short term.Therefore,the current impact can be assessed as low,therefore the TARA framework suggests that we should find risk strategies to 'Reduce' this risk.The most common way to reduce such a risk is through fuel hedging,a contractual tool that airlines use to reduce exposure to the volatility of fuel prices.We can hedge the risk of a possible increase in crude oil prices by purchasing forward contracts,which usually involves engaging in a contract where the parties agree to buy or sell a certain quantity of fuel at a specified price on a future date.This then gives us some degree of certainty to the price that will be paid and therefore reduces the risk of volatility. This risk is one which we must monitor closely,as should fuel price volatility continue,we may need to pass this on to customers in our ticket prices,which could impact on demand and reduce profitability.Therefore,the impact would move towards 'High'and therefore alternative risk management strategies would be needed. Climate risks -Accept As noted in the latest risk assessment report,climate change is an increasing threat to the airline industry,both in terms of potentially increasing costs due to regulation and legislation demands and due to its impact on the climate and the adverse weather conditions this creates.In terms on the consequences/impact on our overall operations,these are currently relatively low.Adverse weather is an increasing issue for all airlines,but it currently does not have significant operational impact on the whole business.It could be disruptive in certain parts of our region but overall,most operations are likely to continue as normal.Legislation and regulatory changes relating to the climate are likely to continue to be a challenge but again,currently their impact on us is not significant. In terms of probability,this is most definitely increasing.We are aware of increasing legislation and increasing adverse weather events.However,in terms of likelihood of adverse conditions severely impacting our overall business operations,the probability is relatively low.Therefore,from the TARA matrix,we would accept this risk as a normal part of our business operations,meaning that we do not necessarily need to take specific risk mitigation action.However,as a responsible organisation,I would recommend that we do proactively monitor and manage this risk and find ways to manage our own impact on the changing climate.This is likely to mean working with governments and regulators to ensure we are working within recommended climate and emissions targets and that we actively monitor weather patterns. Security risks -Avoid The probability of an operational threat to our flights into Sondar is high,considering that civil unrest is increasing and the fact that one of our competitor's flights was hijacked only last week. Although we only fly a limited number of flights to Sondar,as a high-profile business based in Corland,this may be an opportunity for a hostile regime to attack the Corland government. The impact of such a threat occurring could be catastrophic.Although our competitors reported only injuries to crew and staff,there is no way of knowing that such attacks could escalate and the next case could be more dangerous.We cannot allow our staff and passengers to be placed at such risk.Therefore,as both likelihood and consequences are considered to be high,we should avoid this risk altogether and withdraw allservices to Sondar until the political situation stabilises and the threat of disruption becomes manageable.This risk cannot currently be taken. It is worth noting that security is a key risk to our business but in most cases,it is not one that we can avoid,as it is an inevitable aspect of the environment within which we operate.Therefore,in terms of our overallsecurity,we would try to reduce these risks as much as possible through multiple security measures,including robust security systems operating both in-flight and on the ground.We should also transfer much of this risk to industry experts in airport security who operate through the airports that we fly to. I hope that this email is informative and useful for your meeting. The answer was presented in a suitable email format. 0 1.33 2.66 4 To: Corjetz's shareholders From: Senior business analyst Date: September 20X3 Briefing Paper Technological Developments Part 1:Benefits and challenges of data analytics Corjetz applies data analytics extensively throughout the business and considers the data that it generates as a key business resource.We should use this data to help us to maintain our strategic position as an efficient,low-cost airline operator. Benefits · Big data servers allow Corjetz to capture and interrogate on-board flight data relating to the operational performance of the flight and to interrogate the on-board maintenance systems.By capturing in-flight performance data from every flight,Corjetz can identify patterns of performance across the whole fleet to enable potential issues to be spotted early,allowing for early intervention before serious technical faults are developed.This will save costly delays in grounding aircraft when faults are potentially discovered later and could require more costly rectification work.It also ensures that there is limited disruption to schedules and customers, ensuring that we maintain customer satisfaction. · Capturing data from the aircraft maintenance system also helps Corjetz to predict when and where faults are most likely.This will help in being aware and prepared for these events and having alternative aircraft ready and/or having spare parts or necessary technical experts on hand and ready exactly when they are needed.This should reduce down-time and minimise the costs associated with customer disruption. · By using data analytics on our customer data,Corjetz can identify the best performing flights in terms of revenue per seat,load factor and customer spend and therefore we can try to increase the load factor of these flights or potentially update flight schedules to increase the frequency of the most profitable flights. · Analysis of on-board purchasing data willallow us to understand customer buying habits and predict what they will buy on-board.This will give us an opportunity to streamline the on-board service,reducing those items least purchased and ensuring that popular items are well stocked and advertised adequately.This should also minimise waste. · Analysis of how and where customers book their flights will give us the opportunity to target our marketing more effectively.Profiling customer types to certain destinations and flights will also help to streamline our marketing efforts and target customers more effectively through the most effective media channels.This should also improve customer satisfaction. · Effective scheduling(i.e.,offering the right flights to the right places at the right time)is a key component of our business model and data analytics can help us in this process.By being able to predict flights most likely delayed we can put strategies in place to ensure these are minimised,such as working more closely with affected airports and by re-scheduling flights to avoid times when delays are most likely to occur,thus minimising the costs associated with delays. · Analysis of crew locations will assist us in optimising crew rotas and locations to ensure cabin crews are optimised ensuring we are optimising staff usage. Challenges · Big data analytics is highly complex.As an organisation,we are collecting millions of data transactions every day about each aircraft,in terms of its operational performance,in flight statistics,engine performance and maintenance data.In addition to that we collect all the customer transaction data both pre-and in-flight.It is evident therefore that there is a potential for data overload. · Too much information could be as dangerous as not having enough to make effective strategic decisions.So much information could in fact stifle speed of response to time critical operational decisions.Data analysis could be a distraction to our core business if we spend too long analysing the data we collect without meaningful results. · Data analytics requires expertise to interrogate and understand the data effectively.The sheer quantity of data could easily be misinterpreted,and incorrect conclusions drawn from the analysis.Data analytic experts are expensive to train and expensive to retain in the business,as competitors are likely to be challenging to obtain the same expertise. · Collecting,managing and analysing vast quantities of data requires extensive expenditure not only on the experts described above but also the technological systems to collect and store this data.This cost will need to be carefully weighed against the opportunities described above. · Systems security is also a key challenge.We will be holding large volumes of customer specific data and we will be obliged by law to maintain and control this information on behalf of our customers.Such information is an obvious target for malicious attack or espionage and therefore it will be critical to maintain and operate the highest levels of systems security.Any breach could be seriously damaging to our reputation and costly in terms of compensation payments and regulatory fines. Overall,it is likely that the benefits gained from being able to exploit the vast amount of data we hold in our business to support and strengthen our strategic position can outweigh the potential challenges of data analytics. Briefing Paper(continued) Part 2:Potential impact of disruptive technologies on Corjetz It is anticipated that the airline industry will continue to face challenges and opportunities from emerging and disruptive technologies.There are several types of emerging technologies that Corjetz could exploit to support its strategic position. Advanced Robotics There are two potential robotics technologies that we could exploit.Firstly,the customer assistance robots could have two impacts.Firstly,it could enhance customer service at the airports where we operate by providing customers with additional advice and information to make their transit through the airport easier.This should improve customer service.However,we would need to take into consideration the potential for customer resistance to this impersonal approach to customer service delivery and we would need to assess which locations this type of technology would be best suited in terms of customer demographics.From the perspective of Corjetz,we would be able to potentially reduce some of our staffing costs by replacing the ground airport customer facing staff with robots. Pre-flight safety checks of all aircraft before departure is an integral part of flight safety activities. Application of robotic aircraft maintenance systems would enable external aircraft pre-flight checks to be undertaken.This would reduce the number of on-ground maintenance crew that Corjetz would need to employ at each airport.This would reduce the costs associated with operating maintenance crews and would potentially reduce the time taken to undertake external aircraft checks as these would be routinised and pre-programmed and carried out without human distractions.Therefore,this could help to reduce potential delays.However,human ground maintenance staff would still be required to facilitate the robotics and to rectify any issues found by the robots. Internet of Things (loT) The loT is the concept of connecting any device to the Internet and then to other connected devices.This connected network collects and shares data about the way each connected device/ object is used and about the environment around them.The loT platform integrates data from the different devices and applies analytics to share the most valuable information to detect patterns, make recommendations,and detect possible problems before they occur. One possible application for Corjetz is in improving logistics of spare parts.By fitting sensors to the most critical aircraft parts,we would be able to have real time data about performance of these parts and detect when they need replacing.This would be linked directly to key suppliers who could be notified of the need for parts in advance to ensure that waiting times for maintenance/ replacement are reduced or removed completely.This would reduce aircraft downtime and reduce potential costly delays to flights or additional payments to suppliers for emergency or unexpected supply requirements. Another application of loT is in weather forecasting,whereby radar systems can be connected directly to aircraft to give pilots real time weather conditions.This should help Corjetz with route planning and being able to respond more quickly to customers in relation to up to date information on potential delays or re-scheduling.This should improve customer experience and customer service. Artificial Intelligence(AI) Using Al technology means pilots can now be trained partially using Al simulators.As an organisation that employs over 2,000 pilots,training costs are a significant expense both in terms of money and time.Al technology could have the potential to reduce the time taken to train pilots, therefore allowing us to optimise their time availability This could also have an impact on reducing our pilot training costs.However,we would need to look closely into this technology as any reduction in quality of training would impact on the quality of our service and the trust of our customers. By aligning artificial Intelligence (Al)with our marketing platforms,we can direct our marketing budget more effectively to specific channels to attract a specific customer group.Tailored marketing messages can be sent directly to individual customers based upon previous purchasing profiles,which in turn should help to stimulate further expenditure.This targeted marketing approach will assist us in retaining current customers and identifying marketing channels to promote our business most effectively to potential customers within the correct location.This will improve efficiency of marketing spend and retain customers and improve customer satisfaction. Corjetz headline results and cost breakdown 1 1.Introduction Corjetz is a low-cost regional airline business.It was established 20 years ago in Corland,a country located in the continent of Hundria.Over the years,it has positioned itself as one of the world's leading low-cost airlines and is currently the second largest low-cost airline business operating in Hundria (in terms of passenger numbers). Corjetz currently operates nearly 1,000 routes (destinations)in over 30 countries across Hundria. Its head office is based at Near City airport,located in the south of Corland.Hundria is apolitically stable and economically developed continent of the world.Most of the countries in Hundria form a trading bloc which facilitates free trade and free movement of people within it.Each country in Hundria uses its own currency.Corland's currency is the $. Corjetz operates a 'point-to-point'service,meaning that it operates its flights directly between destinations.This means that its passengers do not have to travel to or change flights at a 'hub' airport to reach their destination,which helps Corjetz to reduce its operating costs.It also keeps costs low through its 'no-frills'approach to flight delivery.This approach means that in-flight meals are not provided (unless paid for separately)and includes the automation of a wide range of its processes,including bookings,flight documentation,baggage drop and flight check-in. Additionally,it operates a profitable and popular portfolio of routes between over 130 airports across Hundria. 2.Airline industry information Overview The airline industry encompasses a wide range of businesses called airlines which offer an air transport service to customers.Normally referred to as commercial airlines,these businesses offer scheduled commercial flights which carry both human passengers and cargo, and in most cases are carried out using jet aircrafts.Commercial flights are those which transport passengers from one destination to another with a pre-booked ticket.Non-commercial flights are either operated privately or by the military. The airline industry forms just one sector of the wider aviation industry, which also includes all other aviation-related businesses,such as jet engine suppliers,aircraft manufacturers and aerospace companies. The airline industry directly and indirectly supports global trade,tourism,and economic growth. Therefore,it has had a major influence on globalisation in many other industries. Passenger types A leisure passenger is someone travelling on holiday to stay in a hotel or a holiday resort to relax and/or experience local tourist attractions.A business passenger is someone travelling for work purposes and will normally be attending a business meeting or conference.Both types of passengers may be offered the option to travel first class,business class or economy class by some airlines. First class passengers pay significantly more for their ticket but in return receive a high-quality service,including dedicated airport lounges,large and comfortable in-flight seats and unlimited food and drink provided throughout the flight.Business class passengers pay less than first class passengers for their tickets and receive a slightly lower level of service.Economy class passengers pay even less for their flight ticket than business class passengers.An economy price ticket normally includes a standard seat with limited leg room and standard and limited in-flight food options. Airline types-by service International airlines International airlines are the largest and often the most well-known airlines. International airlines offer global services,carrying leisure and business passengers and cargo over long distances.International airlines usually have multiple airport hubs which provide customers with access to hundreds of global destinations. National airlines National airlines typically focus on offering services within their home country,but many also offeraccess to international destinations.A national airline is likely to have a smaller aircraft fleet than the international airlines and often also offer services to both leisure and business passengers. Regional airlines Regional airlines tend to focus on offering services within specific regions, for example,a continent.In some cases,they provide passenger services to locations not offered by either national or international airlines. Airline types-by business model Full-service carriers Full-service carriers offer a range of pre-flight and on-board services within the price of the ticket.This may include checked-in baggage, in-flight meals,airport lounges and multiple service classes,such as first class,business class and economy class.They usually fly national, international,long-haul,and short-haul flights. Low-cost carriers Low-cost carriers aim to gain a competitive advantage over full-service carriers by reducing costs.This is done in several ways,including having smaller fleets and smaller aircraft,by offering a more limited service and by flying to smaller and less popular airports,where landing and ground handling fees are cheaper. Forcustomers,flights from low-cost carriers are often significantly cheaper. Services such as in-flight meals and baggage allowances will normally not be included in the price of the ticket.Instead,where these facilities are available,customers pay extra for them.Some low-cost carriers offer both economy and business class services. Charter airlines Charter airlines focus on providing passenger services to tourists to a specific destination.In most cases,they do not sell airline tickets directly but instead enter into agreements with tour operators who then take responsibility for finding passengers for them. Cargo airlines The main focus of cargo airlines is the transportation of cargo or freight. Some cargo airlines are subsidiaries or sub-divisions of airlines which also offer passenger services. Hub and spoke versus point-to-point travel Airlines which operate a hub and spoke model fly from one airport into a hub airport,with passengers then connecting from the hub onto a flight to their final destination.Airlines operate this model because it allows them to offer more flights to passengers and is designed to optimise an airline's network coverage.The alternative to this is the point-to-point model,where the airline focuses on providing passengers with a route from origin to final destination.In this model,there is no need to connect passengers through a hub airport,as flights operate directly between cities.This model has proved popular with airline customers in recent years and most of the low-cost airlines operate this model. POINT-TO-POINT POINT-TO-POINT Key air transport statistics 20X2 Key statistics People transported (passengers per year) Average number of commercial flights per day Passenger revenues HUB-AND-SPOKE HUB-AND-SPOKE 20X2 Global 3 billion 73,000 $390 billion $79·2 billion20X2 Hundria $79·2 billion 15,000600 million 15,000 Flight management activities Flight management activities are provided by airports.Airlines pay for these services via airport fees. Air traffic control (ATC) Air traffic control (ATC)is a vital aspect of air transportation.It is carried out by ground-based personnel specially trained in monitoring air traffic in a specific location.ATC is responsible for moving aircraft safely and efficiently through the airspace system.Air traffic controllers ensure that aircraft remain at set distances apart whilst moving them between airports along set flight paths. Aircraft ground handling Ground handling refers to the wide range of services provided to facilitate passengers within an airport and to facilitate an aircraft's ground positioning both before and after a flight.Ground handling services include: - Baggage management-including sorting passenger baggage and loading and unloading baggage onto and off aircraft. - Customer service management -including gate assignment,and pre-flight facilities management (such as operating shops and waiting areas in airports). Aircraft boarding management -including operating shuttle bus services between the airport and the aircraft,ramp facilities and passenger travel documentation management. Aircraft towing and re-fuelling services -including movement of an aircraft to or from a parking stand or from one gate to another and the re-fuelling of aircrafts using fuel purchased by the airline. Airport security-including pre-flight passenger screening and security checks,baggage scanning,passport control and all on-site security management activities carried out to protect airport buildings,runways,and machinery. Industry challenges Demand is cyclical,with demand for flights driven by economic growth.In the last ten years, low-cost carriers have increasingly taken market share from full-service carriers by offering passengers affordable and low-cost air travel.This has made air travel more accessible to a much wider customer base globally.It has also introduced a high level of competition to the industry. The airline industry has been impacted by several geopolitical events in recent years,such as terror attacks,cyber-attacks and extreme weather events caused by climate change.These have led to both short and long-term consequences for demand.Terror and cyber-attacks have impacted on customer confidence in the industry and climate change is increasingly prominent on the global political agenda. Fuel is one of the biggest costs for airlines,and one of the most volatile.Fuel represents over 20% of most airlines'cost base annually.The price of jet fuel is strongly correlated to the international price of crude oil which has been highly volatile in the last five years.Fuel costs are particularly prone to foreign exchange movements,which can also have a significant impact on this major cost. The airline industry is still some way away from operating aircraft with alternative fuels such as biofuels,and most airlines are still fully reliant on crude oil.Governments and customers are becoming increasingly environmentally sensitive and the expectation for airlines to operate in a 'greener'way is increasing.If the airline industry is to survive in the long term,it will need to invest significantly in alternative fuel sources. Environmental factors are a major and increasingly significant issue for the airline industry, affecting both passenger demand and operating parameters.Customers are increasingly aware that their carbon footprint from airline travel is contributing around 3%of global carbon emissions. Therefore,many are actively considering alternatives to air travel. Despite environmental concerns,commercial international air traffic(aircraft operating in the skies)increased by 5%in 20X2.According to the latest industry data,the international air traffic control environment is struggling to cope with increasing flight numbers,evidenced by the number of delayed flights rising by nearly 20%in 20X2.Similarly,airports around the world are struggling to cope with demand from airlines for flight slots1.This is leading to increased congestion at airports,resulting in more delays for passengers.This is also driving up aircraft ground handling service charges for all airlines. 1Aflight slot is the authorisation to either land or take off at a particular airport on a particular day during a specified time period.Flight slots are paid for by the airlines. Industry key performance indicators(KPls) Airlines use multiple performance metrics to measure and monitor their performance. Some examples are provided in the table below: KPI Definition 1.Available seat kilometres(ASK) ASK is calculated by taking the number of seats available and multiplying by the distance flown.It is a measure of airline capacity. 2.Revenue per available seat kilometre(RASK) RASK is calculated by dividing the airline's total revenue by its total ASK.It is a measure of potential revenue generation. 3.Load factor Calculated as the percentage of checked-in passengers in relation to an aircraft's available seats.It is a measure of utilisation. 4.Revenue passenger kilometres(RPK) RPK is calculated as the number of passengers multiplied by the total distance travelled.It shows the number of kilometres travelled by paying passengers who are referred to as 'revenue passengers'. 5.CO₂per passenger (grams) Calculated as total CO₂produced by the airline divided by the number of passengers. 6.Punctuality The percentage of flights which land on time at the destination airport.Typically,this means landing within 15 minutes of the scheduled arrival time. Industry body The airline industry has a global trade association.Its role is to help formulate industry policy and support airline activity.It promotes awareness of the benefits which the airline industry brings to national and global economies.Its role is also to develop global standards for the air transport industry,and it provides advice on airline safety and security by setting clearly defined rules. 3.Corjetz overview Background Corjetz was established in Corland 20 years ago by entrepreneurial brothers Eric and Axel Madson.They both had worked for many years in the airline industry and had a keen interest in travelling.With money inherited from their parents,they acquired two second-hand aircraft.They started by operating flights from Near City airport,located 40 miles from Corland's capital city in the south of the country,to airports located close to two cities in the north of the country. Using their knowledge and experience of the airline industry,Eric and Axel spotted a gap in the market in Corland for more convenient and affordable low-cost air travel.Up to that point, Corland's airline industry was dominated by full-service carriers. The low-cost business model in the Corland airline industry proved extremely popular with air passengers,who liked the convenience of Corjetz's point-to-point service and the significantly cheaper ticket prices than those offered by existing full-service carriers. Over the years,Corjetz has focused on operating flights to and from airports across Hundria with the highest customer demand and continually looks for opportunities to extend its network.Cost efficiency has been its overriding strategic focus.This has been delivered through developing and maintaining long-term strategic partnerships with aircraft manufacturers,key airports,ground handling operators and maintenance contractors who maintain Corjetz's owned aircrafts,and by operating the latest and most efficient aircraft. Over the last 20 years the low-costmodel of air travel has continuedBrygair(Broland)continent.Corjetz(Corland)14%LKK(Lakland)to be very popular with customersacross Hundria,and there are now 30Low cost airline market shareHundria 20X2(passenger numbers)low-cost airlines operating across theOthers(27 airlines)60%17%9% Over the last 20 years the low-cost model of air travel has continued Brygair (Broland) continent. Corjetz (Corland) 14% LKK (Lakland) to be very popular with customers across Hundria,and there are now 30 Low cost airline market share Hundria 20X2(passenger numbers) low-cost airlines operating across the Others (27 airlines) 60% 17% 9% Eric and Axel both retired from the business 10 years ago but since their departure,Corjetz has remained focused on maintaining and strengthening its low-cost strategic position. Corjetz growth 1100 300 200 100 Routes(Destinations) 10 YEARSIN OPERATION 20 Ownership Corjetz was listed on the Corland stock market nine years ago,one year after Eric and Axel retired.The brothers originally retained a 35%share in the business when it was first listed but since then,they have sold most of their shareholdings in Corjetz and they no longer play any role in running the business.Currently,80%of Corjetz's shares are held by large institutional investors,10%of shares are held by Corjetz employees and the remaining shares are held by small private investors.Magda Fisk is the current CEO of Corjetz and has been in place for 10 years.She was instrumental in achieving Corjetz's listing. Corjetz key resources Routes Corjetz has a profitable and extensive portfolio of routes which it operates to and from over 130 airports across Hundria.This makes it very popular with customers,who find the range of destinations and frequency of flights offered by Corjetz very convenient and attractive.It regularly reviews its route network to exploit the latest demand opportunities. Fleet Corjetz operates a modern fleet of 300 aircraft,of which 75%are owned outright and the remaining 25%are leased (leasing is a common form of financing in the airline industry).Corjetz has invested significantly in the latest aircraft which are more fuel efficient and environmentally friendly.This has contributed significantly to its low-cost strategic position and has also positively impacted on reducing carbon emissions.Corjetz's average aircraft is 4·5 years old,which is low compared to the industry standard. Corjetz focuses on leisure passengers rather than on business passengers,therefore its aircraft cabins consist of economy class seats only with a high-density layout (this gives Corjetz 150 seats per aircraft compared to 140 seats on similar sized aircraft operated by its competitors). This higher seat density allows Corjetz to offer lower ticket prices per flight which is attractive to customers. Technology Corjetz applies data analytics extensively throughout its business and considers data to be a key business resource.It has always been an early adopter of the latest technologies in the airline industry.It currently makes extensive use of technology throughout its business operations, including: - A mobile app which allows passengers to book flights,check in for flights,download boarding passes (to either print off or store as a mobile boarding pass on the passenger's smartphone)and receive real-time flight status information.The app significantly reduces the cost of issuing,distributing and processing physical tickets.An enhanced feature of the mobile app is the integration of live data from the airport's information systems with the passenger's booking and flight details,allowing personalised instructions and updates to be sent to passengers before their journey. In most airports Corjetz operates fully automated baggage check-in machines to help reduce check-in queues. All aircraft are fitted with electronic touchpads for the crew,to replace laptops and printed navigational charts for pilots.This has replaced the need for each flight to carry heavy printed flight logbooks which has helped to reduce CO₂emissions per flight. AIl Corjetz's aircraft are fitted with big data servers to collect continuous in-flight data about flight and engine performance. Corjetz continually searches fornew technologies toenhanceandsupport its business operations, to improve efficiency and reduce costs. People Corjetz employs nearly 11,800 staff,including over 2,000 pilots and 6,500 cabin crew.The remaining staff fulfila number of roles,including staff operating at airports and head office staff operating in IT,finance,customer services and human resource management.Regular updates are provided to employees on key business issues which include a regularly updated intranet, a monthly 'meet the board'online chat,and a weekly staff newsletter.All Corjetz employees undergo a full induction programme and have access to online learning resources,as well as career development opportunities.All employees receive regular and scheduled managerial appraisal of their performance. Board structure Corjetz has six executive directors and five NEDs(including the chair).The board structure is as follows: Non-executive chairSeniorNon-executive directordirectorChief people officerNon-executive Non-executiveChiefcommercialofficerChiefinformationofficerdirector directorChiefmarketingofficerChief finance officerChiefexecutive officerindependentnon-executive Non-executive chair Senior Non-executive director director Chief people officer Non-executive Non-executive Chief commercial officer Chief information officer director director Chief marketing officer Chief finance officer Chief executive officer independent non-executive There are four sub-committees: Nomination committee一 Audit committee Nomination committee Remuneration committee Remuneration committee 一 Safety committee Risk management and key risks Corjetz has a detailed risk register,and a robust enterprise risk management (ERM)framework is in place.The board takes responsibility for determining the nature and extent of the risks it is willing to take to achieve its strategic objectives.It is also responsible for maintaining and monitoring the systems of internal control and risk management.Risk management activities are co-ordinated by the risk and assurance team,led by a risk manager who reports to both the chief financial officer,and to the chair of the audit committee.Corjetz's key risks include: Safety and security of customers and staff.Delivering safe and secure operations which meet the needs and expectations of customers and staff is critical to the business. The growing impact of climate change and increasing expectations from customers, governments,regulators,and wider society for a greater focus on the environment and sustainability activities. Macro-economic and geopolitical events such as general economic trends,foreign exchange rates and volatile fuel prices which all impact financial performance. The availability,security and performance of its website and its critical business operating systems. 4.Corjetz website extracts What we do What we do We consistently deliver low fares to our customers and operate flights to the places our customers most want to travel to,using our cost advantage and leading position at airports across Hundria Mission statementTo connect the people of Hundria,through affordable and sustainable air travel. This includes allowing school children to train using the academy's facilities and play in the academy's stadium.The facilities wil also be avalable for hiring out on a commercial basis. Financing the construction The cost of the new academy is estimated to be $120 million.This will be fully financed by the sale of the land which is adjacent to AT's stadium,which Freenia's council is about to vacate as its lease ends. Non-executive director We must bear in mind that our commitment to stakeholders is part of our values.This means that we must treat stakeholders fairly and equitably.If there are conflicts between stakeholders,we need to give them some,but not all, of what they want. Finance director We cannot ignore the influence stakeholders have when deciding how we fulfil their demands.We have to satisfy the demands of some stakeholders in order to continue to exist.For example,our men's first team willbe expelled from the Kyleland football league if we fail to fulfil its regulations.We are contractually obliged to meet the demands of the television company.We should pay less attention to less important stakeholders.Our priority in dealing with the local community should be to obey local laws.We don't need to worry further about their complaints about noise and disturbance on matchdays.We also shouldn't worry about criticisms made by supporters on social media.The individuals making these comments have no power by themselves over AT. Marketing and It director Taking the approach the finance director suggests would completely undermine the efforts we're currently making to use social media to improve relationships with our stakeholders,particularly our supporters.We should focus on enhancing two-way communication to develop relations with stakeholders.Stakeholders want insight into what is happening at AT and to be sure we are listening to their views. Chair I also have concerns about damage to our reputation,as this can adversely affect relations with stakeholders.We need to be aware of threats to our reputation,for example,players behaving badly or AT not acting in line with its mission.We must consider the impacts of these threats if they materialise. 批注[9]:董事会主席： 我想询问你关于我们如何管理利益相关者的意见。如果我 们的利益相关者不满意我们所做的事情，我们可能会遭受 负面公众舆论或媒体报导。我们必须建立明确的原则来管 理利益相关者，并处理利益相关者需求之间的冲突。 批注[10]:非执行董事： 我们必须牢记，我们对利益相关者的承诺是我们价值观的 一部分。这意味着我们必须公平、公正地对待利益相关者。 如果利益相关者之间存在冲突，我们需要给予他们一定的 满足，但不能满足所有要求。 批注[11]:财务总监： 在决定我们如何满足利益相关者的需求时，我们不能忽视 他们的影响影响。为了继续经营，我们必须满足某些利益 相关者的需求。例如，如果我们不遵守Kyleland 足球联赛 的规定，我们的男子一线队将被逐出该联赛。我们有合同 义务满足电视公司的要求。我们应该较少关注不太重要的 利益相关者。我们在处理当地社区事务时的首要任务应该 是遵守当地法律。我们不必进一步担心他们在比赛日对噪 音和干扰的投诉。我们也不必担心球迷在社交媒体上的批 评。发表这些评论的个人本身对AT没有影响力。 批注[12]:市场、IT 总监：
