Financial benefits Income from player development In the longer-term,developing players can improve AT's financial position.Having a bigger nucleus of players available from within AT will mean that fewer players have to be bought in from outside,saving transfer fees and possibly the high wages necessary to attract players from other clubs.Players can also be sold to other clubs,generating transfer fee income. Other income The facilities are a potential source of income.They could be used by other clubs and sports, generating cash flow during football's close season.The lecture facilities can also be used for conferences. Naming The academy provides an opportunity for sale of naming rights.Sponsoring the facilities may appeal to commercial sponsors who will be able to access the facilities and also be associated with the likely to be for the Saving hiring costs development longer-term. and education of young players.Any such arrangement is Hiring other faclities is an expense which does not benefit AT.AT also does not have control over the facilities of others which it uses. 批注[31]:回答内容结合exhibits1, 注意 report 格式 introduction 简单抄题 正文部分框架为： Financial benefits Financial drawback Non-financial benefits Non-financial drawback 批注[32]:财务好处 球员发展收入 从长远来看，发展球员可以改善AT的财务状况。拥有一 个更大的来自俱乐部内部的核心球员意味着更少的球员需 要从外部购买，节省了转会费，也可能节省了从其他俱乐 部吸引球员所需的高额工资。球员也可以卖给其他俱乐部， 产生转会费收入。 其他收入 这些设施是潜在的收入来源。它们可以被其他俱乐部和体 育项目使用，在足球赛季结束时产生现金流。课堂设施也 可用于会议。 冠名 学院提供了一个出售冠名权的机会。赞助这些设施可以吸 引商业赞助商，这些赞助商将能够使用这些设施，并且还 与年轻球员的发展和教育相关联。任何此类安排都可能是 长期的。 节省租赁成本 租用其他设施是一笔毫无益处的开支。AT也不能控制它 所使用的其他设备。 Financial drawbacks Difficulty of analysis There is no estimation of the financial benefits of developing the academy.Ideally AT should make a comparison between the significant expense of establishing and running the academy,and the financial benefits. However,any financial benefits from developing players are likely to be for the longer-term and very difficult to estimate.An estimate could be made of future transfer fees based on how other clubs have gained income from the sale of players in their academies.However, this assumes the same success rate of developing players at AT's academy and estimates of future transfer fees,which are uncertain.It is more difficult to estimate the impact of not buying players in,as that presupposes knowledge of future playing staff strategies,as wellas future transfer fees.This makes conventional investment appraisal analysis very difficult Even if a return figure could be estimated,there is also the issue of what a satisfactory return on the investment would be.Again,this would have to be considered over the longer- term,but over what time-period is difficult to determine. Other sources of income The facilities'generation of other income may also be doubtful.AT faces considerable competition in obtaining conference and hospitality income.Any commercial activities would have to be fitted round AT's activities,or AT may need to adjust its activities if facilities were hired out.The capacity of the training stadium may be too small for other events or sports. Alternative use of funds The academy is likely to benefit AT in the longer-term.However,currently AT is having trouble competing with other clubs,partly because of its lower stadium capacity generating lower receipts.A more urgent use for funds would appear to be for either a new stadium or to increase the capacity of the current stadium.There may also be a need for more expendture on the current stadium to fulfi stricter health and safety requirements 批注[33]:财务弊端 分析难度 发展该学院的财政收益无法估计。理想情况下， AT 应在 建立和运营学院的巨额费用和财务收益之间进行比较。 然而，发展球员的任何经济利益都可能是长期的，并且很 难估计。可以根据其他俱乐部如何从出售他们学院的球员 中获得收入来估计未来的转会费。然而，这是假设在AT 学院发展球员的成功率和未来转会费的估计相同，但这是 不确定的。很难估计不买球员的影响，因为这需要知道未 来球员的策略，以及未来的转会费。这使得传统的投资评 估分析非常困难。 即使可以估算出回报数字，也存在一个问题就是令人满意 的投资回报到底是多少。这必须从更长远的角度来考虑， 但在多长时间内很难确定。 其他收入来源 这些设施产生的其他收入也值得怀疑。AT 在获取会议和 招待收入方面面临着激烈的竞争。任何商业活动都必须围 绕AT的活动进行，或者如果设施被出租， AT可能需要调 整其活动。训练场馆的容量对于其他赛事或运动来说可能 太 小 。 资金的其他用途 从长远来看，该学院可能会从中受益。然而，目前AT在 与其他俱乐部的竞争中遇到了麻烦，部分原因是其较低的 球场容量导致收入较低。资金的更紧急用途似乎是建造一 个新的体育场或增加现有体育场的容量。可能还需要在现 有体育场上投入更多资金，以满足更严格的健康和安全要 求。 Non-financial benefits Attraction of more players The facilities at the academy and a more comprehensive development programme will make it more likely that AT willproduce high quality players.This should attract more players from a wider community than currently.It will help AT compete better with other clubs in sining young players.The emphasis on player safeguarding and welfare,and all-round education, should help convince young players'parents that their children should join the academy. Mission and corporate social responsibility The stadium can be used to enhance links to the local community by providing the facilities free of charge.This is in line with AT's commitment in its values to the community and publicising it can enhance AT's reputation.Use by local schools and children's teams also creates links and enhances the chances that promising players will join AT's'academy. Forward planning Having a regular stream of young players coming through from the academy into the first team should enable longer-term planning.AT's management should be able to anticipate how younger players will be able to fill the gaps as older players retire or leave AT when their contracts expire.This should reduce the need for AT to have to enter the transfer market when there are gaps,helping stability. Educating players Developing players in the academy enables AT to promote its way of doing things.This does not just apply to the way AT plays football but wider values,including integrity and commitmentThis should hopefully reduce the risk of ethical threats,such as bribery.Having many first-team players who have come through the academy is likely to improve AT's overall culture. Enhanced relationship with players As players who have come through the academy have been at AT since they were children, they may be more oyal to AT.There is likely to be a stronger relationship between the players as they have known each other longer and understand how to play together, resulting in better teamworkAs the players have been developed by AT,team management should have a good understanding of these players'strengths and weaknesses. 批注[34]:非财务益处 吸引更多球员 学院的设施和更全面的发展计划将使AT 更有可能培养出 高质量的球员。这将比现在吸引更多的球员。这将有助于 在培养年轻球员方面更好地与其他俱乐部竞争。强调球员 的安全和福利，以及全面的教育，有助于说服年轻球员的 父母让孩子加入青训学院。 使命和企业社会责任 这个体育场可以通过免费提供设施来加强与当地社区的联 系。这符合AT 对社区的价值观承诺，宣传它可以提高AT 的声誉。当地学校和儿童队的使用也建立了联系，增强了 有前途的球员加入学院的机会。 未来规划 有一批来自青训学院的年轻球员进入一线队，应该可以进 行更长期的规划。AT 的管理层应该能够预测年轻球员将 如何填补老球员退役或合同到期后离开AT 的空缺。这应 该会减少AT 在转会市场出现缺口时不得不进入的需求， 有利于稳定。 球员教育 在青训学院培养球员使AT 能够推广其做事方式。这不仅 适用于踢球的方式，也适用于更广泛的价值观，包括诚信 和承诺。这有望降低道德威胁的风险，如贿赂。拥有许多 来自青训学院的一线队球员可能会改善 AT的整体文化。 增强与球员的关系 由于从青训学院出来的球员从小就在AT, 他们可能对AT 更忠诚。球员之间可能会有更强的关系，因为他们彼此认 识的时间更长，并且知道如何一起打球，从而产生更好的 团队合作。由于球员都是AT培养出来的，球队管理层应 该对这些球员的优缺点有很好的了解。 Non-financial drawbacks Location The academy would be completey separate from the main club and located some distance awayThere will inevitably be less contact between players in the first team and the academy,and it may make it more diffcult to integrate the academy's players into the rest of AT.If the location is out-of-town,it may be hard to hire out the facilities. Selling players to other clubs If the academy predominately produces players who are sold to other clubs,AT may gain the reputation for investing in player development but then realising their value rather than using them to enhance its first team.Although AT may obtain significant income from transfer fees,it may indicate to supporters and commercial sponsors that AT lacks ambition. It may suggest AT prefers to receive funds from sales rather than utilise the players developed to improve competitiveness. Recommendation Whilst the academy development willbenefit AT in the longer-term,it seems that expenditure on the current or a new stadium may be a more urgent priority.If investmentis made in the academy now,AT may not have the funds to enhance capacity and may fall further behind other clubs.Theidea of the more focused approach to development that the academy would offer is a good one,but AT should consider if an academy can be developed more cheaply than investingin the purpose-built site. Proposed strategies Non-executive director The non-executive director's suggestion is one of satisficingacknowledging that stakeholder conflicts will exist and it is likely to be impossible to satisfy every stakeholder completely. This policy has the advantage of fairness,that once a stakeholder's claim is acknowledged as legitimate,they have rights in relation to AT which need to be satisfied to some degree. However,the director does not suggest how AT should determine what criteria should be used if stakeholder interests come into conflict to determine which stakeholder should be satsfied and which disappointed.The approach does not consider the extent to which stakeholder requirements are in the best interests of AT,or whether they are unreasonable. The views also take no account of the power and interest which stakeholders have.In particular,t will be necessary to meet the requirements of some particularly powerful stakeholders even if others are disappointed. Finance director The finance director's views essentially address the non-executive director's views,in adopting the strategic positioning approach and using the criteria of level of power and interest to determine stakeholder policy.Keeping key players happy-those with high power and interest-has the vital advantage of guaranteeing AT's existence.It will mean that AT focuses on what it needs to do,for example fulfil league regulations and contractual obligations with commercial sponsors and broadcasters. Patterns Analysis of social media activity can provide patterns in data in relation to customer requirements,as regards matchday experience and the products it sells.This can be used to determine what AT should do,if,for example,there are demands for faclities to be more family friendly.Good or poor reactions to products can help determine future stocking decisions.Seeing patterns may also help predict future behaviour. If AT's data protection precautions are inadequate,it could suffer legal action and fines under data protection legislation.It could also be sued by supporters and customers whose data has been accessed. If AT's failure to protect data is publicised,customers and supporters will not be willing to trust it with their personal data.This will jeopardise AT's initiatives to develop closer relations with supporters.If customers are unwilling to do business with AT on-line,this may threaten income streams.Any breaches may well be widely publicised,given the media attention on AT,and this may threaten its reputation and hence harm its brand. Actions AT needs to review its IT infrastructure to see if responsibility for data protection controls is clear and has been communicated.The marketing and IT director needs to take ultimate charge of it but be supported by personnel with specific security responsibilities.AT needs to ensure that its procedures and documentation are up-to-date and in line with data protection regulations,that staff have been adequately trained in security procedures and the requirements of data protection regulations.The external IT environment needs to be monitored and systems updated to counter new threats. Specific controls protecting data include encrypting sensitive data such as supporters'or customers'personal details.Access to sensitive data should also be restrcted by passwords and two-factor authentication to as limited a number of staff as possible.Controls over recruitment should ensure that appropriate references are obtained for staff who have roles in relation to sensitive data Contingency arrangements should also be in place to ensure that data is properly backed up and secured.A plan is needed for situations where breaches have occurred,aiming to protect data as far as possible and contact those affected. 批注[88]:数据安全(保护)风险 批注[89]:影响 网络安全漏洞可能导致数据丢失。还可能导致未经授权的 数据访问，包括AT自己的数据以及有关其客户和球迷的 数据 。 批注[90]:如果AT的数据保护预防措施不足，它可能会根 据数据保护立法遭受法律诉讼和罚款。它也可能被球迷和 客户起诉，因为他们的数据遣受访问。 批注[91]:如果AT未能保护数据而被大家知道，客户和球 迷将不愿意将他们的个人数据托付给AT。这将危及 AT 与 球迷建立更紧密关系的举措。如果客户不愿意与AT在线 开展业务，这可能会威胁到收入流。鉴于媒体对AT的关 注，任何违规行为都可能被广泛宣传，这可能会威胁到其 声誉，从而损害其品牌。 批注[92]:采取的措施 AT 需要审查其IT 基础设施，以查看数据保护控制的责任 是否明确并已传达。营销和T 总监承担最终职责，但需 要由具有特定安全职责的人员提供支持。AT需要确保其 程序和文档是最新的，并符合数据保护监管规定，需要确 保员工在安全程序和数据保护监管要求方面接受了充分的 培训。需要监控外部IT 环境，并更新系统以应对新威胁。 批注[93]:保护数据的特定控制措施包括加密敏感数据， 例如球迷或客户的个人详细信息。还应通过密码和双因素 身份验证限制对敏感数据的访问，并尽可能限制有权限员 工的数量。对招聘的控制应确保从事敏感数据相关角色的 员工需要获得恰当的推荐。 批注[94]:还应制定应急安排，以确保数据得到适当的备 份和保护。针对已经发生泄露的情况，需要制定计划，旨 在尽可能地保护数据并联系受影响的人。 Task 3 A report has just been received from the independent investigator hired to look into the allegations of bribery. Suggested Solution Requirement 1(a) Slide 1 Key entrepreneurial qualities of Corjetz's founders 1.Risk taking 2.Innovative 3.Visionary Leadership 4.Strong Product knowledge Key entrepreneurial qualities of Corjetz's founders Ability to take a risk Starting a new venture such as a new airline involved a huge amount of risk of failure.The founders could not be certain that the low-cost carrier approach would be taken up by customers, therefore the risks were high. Innovation The founders had a completely new approach to air travel in a very well-established market.Their low-cost proposition achieved the same thing as the traditional carriers but in a more efficient and economical way. Visionary leadership To be successful,entrepreneurs should have a clear vision of their new venture.The founders of Corjetz had a vision of the direction of the airline industry today.They were inspirational leaders who guided employees towards this direction. Strong product knowledge The owners knew the air transport market very well and were knowledgeable of the latest trends in the market.It is essential for them to know whether the new low-cost service would meet the demands of the market and to know when it is time for change. (ii)Slide 2 Leadership style shaping culture of innovation ·Culture of innovation ·Freedom to make decisions and make mistakes · Inclusion-make use of operational knowledge ·Celebrate success/history-stories ·Team building and brainstorming ·Control and risk management focus Corjetz culture Corjetz has a culture of innovation,which was established from the very start,as it was the first low-cost airline operator in Corland.This drive for innovation is assisted by its adoption of innovative technologies and through its overall low-cost business strategy. Leadership style and culture The leaders of Corjetz try to encourage the innovation culture by giving staff the freedom to make their own decisions and to make mistakes.Innovation is unlikely if staff feel constrained or afraid to challenge the norm to find new ways of operating. The leaders also encourage leadership and participation in decision making.Staff are the ones working on the front line and will have the most experience to challenge current ways of operating and find new ways.By encouraging participation,they are more likely to put forward ideas for innovation.The leaders recognise and value the expertise of the staff they employ,which will encourage staff to come forward with innovative ideas and opportunities. Corjetz's leaders also celebrate and share successes in the business.This is an important part of the cultural web,as shared successes will encourage staff to want to feel part of that success and therefore find ways to innovate,Team-building and brainstorming events are key routines in the business that also help to encourage a culture of innovation. Also sharing the past history of the business is an important way of focusing staff on what Corjetz does and how it has got its position and to celebrate that journey of innovation.Stories are an important part of encouraging buy-in to the culture. Leadership expects full commitment to the strong focus on control and risk management and that the business operates with the highest standards of safety.Therefore,there is stilla strong leadership focus on control and risk management,but this is done within the context of the innovative way in which Corjetz operates. Requirement 1(b) To: Chief people officer From: Senior business analyst Date: September 20X3 Briefing Notes lmportance of Demonstrating CSR Importance to Corjetz Attracting and retaining customers Consumers are increasingly aware of the importance of social responsibility,and some actively seek products and services from businesses that operate responsibly.CSR demonstrates that we are a business that takes an interest in wider social issues particularly the impact of the airline industry on the environment,rather than just those issues that impact profit margins.An environmentally aware stance willattract customers who share the same values. Contributing towards the communities where our customers live and work makes good business sense and enables us to operate sustainably. Attract/retain employees Socially responsible businesses attract employees who are eager to make a difference in the world.Employees enjoy working more for organisations that have a positive public image than one that doesn't.By showing that you're committed to things like human rights,you're much more likely to attract and retain the top candidates.The workplace willbe more positive and productive, and by encouraging staff to work in the community,we are promoting personal and professional growth. Proving to staff that we are committed to them,through talent management programmes and through offering them opportunities to contribute to their own communities is a good way to enhance staff loyalty and commitment to the business and its goals. Improving the bottom line for shareholders Socially responsible companies create positive brand recognition which increases customer loyalty and should attract high quality employees.These are the basis of us achieving increased profitability and long-term financial success,which will be important to our investor.Also,many other changes in favour of sustainability,such as using less plastic,creating less waste and minimising the use of non-sustainable resources such as fossil fuels,will help to decrease costs. Improved image in the community Consumers are increasingly assessing organisations'public image when deciding whether to buy from them.By engaging in activities such as charitable donations and staff members volunteering at charities and community projects,demonstrates that the business is committed to helping others which is important to our public image.Being committed to ethical practices,will hopefully generate good publicity and more people will hear about our brand,which creates an increased brand awareness. An advantage over competitors By embracing CSR,we can try to stand out from our competitors in the airline industry. Establishing ourselves as a company committed to going one step further than other airline operators in considering our social and environmental impacts in how we operate,sets us apart. However,we must actively deliver on these differences and not just pay lip service to them in order to maintain this competitive edge. Strategic proposals The following proposals will be evaluated below: 1.Route expansion in Hundria 2.Offer business class seats on flights Proposal 1:Route expansion in Hundria As we will be operating exclusively within the Hundrian market and offering the same low-cost flights to customers(i.e.,the same product),then this option would be a market penetration strategy.Market penetration is likely to be the least risky growth strategy to adopt,as clearly,we have vast experience in the Hundrian market,and we are merely proposing to offer customers 'more of the same'.There is evidence of demand for more flights within Hundria and there are more areas of this market that Corjetz could exploit.Therefore,it is suitable in terms of our current strategic position.However there a couple of points that we would need to consider,relating to this strategy. Firstly,we would need to undertake more market research into the demand for flights to the cities where we do not currently fly to.These may not be viable destinations for large numbers of our customers and therefore flights offered my not be full and therefore not financially viable. Secondly,although research suggests that there is a high demand for flights to destinations where we only fly for a limited number of months,there is also the issue of airport capacity.Increasing the number of flights may not be achievable in some of these locations if airport capacity is already under pressure.Also,more flights into busier airports are likely to mean potential delays for passengers and a lower quality of on-ground service,which impacts on our own reputation.We would have to undertake extensive research and consultation with airports to make sure that quality of service could still be achieved for our customers.We would also have to consider the additional costs of airport fees and marketing costs to increase customer awareness of our expanded flights. Proposal 2:Offer business class seats on flights The CCO has proposed that Corjetz should offer a business class service to existing destinations. This is an example of a product development strategy,as we are offering a new product(business class service)to our existing destinations within Hundria. Market research suggests that there is a growing demand for business class service on flights in Hundria and as we have huge experience in this location,then expanding our offerings to include business class travel would seem a sensible development opportunity.In addition,business class passengers would pay double the price for a seat compared to economy class passengers,but this would of course mean that the quality and level of service offered would have to be increased. Aircraft would need to be re-configured to accommodate both business and economy class passengers and there would have to be a discernible difference between the services that we offer.As we have no experience of delivering business class services,significant research would be needed to ascertain their specific needs to ensure that we could differentiate our business class delivery from current,more experienced,competitors. As we are late to the business class travel market,and we are not known for this type of service delivery,it may take significant marketing campaigns to convince current passengers that we can deliver such services to the same standard as other business class service operators.Current operators are likely to react aggressively to such a move and are likely to put forward their own aggressive marketing campaigns to retain their customers.This could be damaging to us and highly expensive to present similar marketing initiatives.We would have to weigh up the costs and benefits of such a campaign. One of the biggest advantages is that we can charge a premium for business class tickets. However,for this price we must deliver to customer expectations,as should we fail,it could damage our reputation. Recommendation Both proposals have merits and definite potential for positive growth for Corjetz.However,we need to weigh up these benefits against the potential costs and timeframes for delivery.Both proposals would seem viable for growth within the given timescales,but we would need significant research specifically in the business class service delivery proposal before acquiring/leasing aircraft configured to deliver this service.Proposal 1 would probably seem to be the least risky option at this time and one which could still deliver growth in our target market. Financing The first financing option alternative is to take on more debt.As a business we are currently highly geared,but this is normal in the airline industry,and we are currently below the industry average. It is unlikely that the additional 25 aircraft(which is approximately an additional 8%to our current fleet of aircraft)will make a significant impact on our current gearing level.However,we have strict debt covenants on our existing debt,and we will need to carefully assess whether more debt is viable.However,we have a sound relationship with current debt providers and high debt is a common feature of the airline industry and therefore obtaining more debt may be a viable option. The second option is to lease the 25 aircraft.We currently lease 25%of our fleet which is a total of 75 aircraft.This arrangement would increase our leased percentage of aircraft to 32.5%of total aircraft(100 out of 325 aircraft).Leasing is a common arrangement in the airline industry,and we are familiar with this financing arrangement with our current lease partner.The aircraft willbe available in the desired timeline and are similar to the aircraft we already operate.Should we choose to deliver proposal 2 we will need to ensure that our lease partner can readily convert these aircraft to deliver both business and economy class travel facilities.However,this would appear to be a readily available and viable financing option Recommendation Based on the above analysis and considering our current financing position in regard to other aircraft,I would suggest opting for a leasing arrangement with our current lease partner.This option would enable us to maintain our healthy liquidity position and would also ensure our gearing remains stable.We have established relationships with our current lease partners and therefore this option is likely to cause least disruption and will be straightforward to arrange as we already have established relationships with the lease partner. Requirement 2b To: Chair of the Audit Committee <Auditcommitteechair@Corjetz.com> From: Senior business analyst <SBA@Corjetz.com> Date: September 20X3 Subject:Risk Assessment Update Dear Chair l am responding on behalf of the chief executive officer who has asked me to present you with an explanation of the information contained in the recent email from the risk manager. Firstly,let me briefly explain the framework currently used by the Risk and Assurance team to assess the organisations risks.This matrix is known as the TARA(or sometimes SARA) framework. The TARA framework This is a tool which can help us assess and manage risks.The framework defines four scenarios based on whether the likelihood and the impact of a risk is high or low.The latest risk assessment carried out by the Risk and Assurance team identified four current risks which they considered to be increasing.We can use the TARA framework to assist in identifying ways to manage these risks effectively. Cyber risks -Transfer Threats from cyber-attacks on our critical operating systems are an ever-present and increasing threat to the business.The impact/consequence of such a threat could be catastrophic, particularly if our in-flight systems were breached,threatening the life of our passengers and staff. Similarly,any downtime in our key operating systems could lead to serious delays or loss of data which could impact on our business operations and ultimately our reputation. Although the probability of these threats is increasing,largely due to the sophistication of hacking techniques and because of the reliance we continue to place on the effective operation of our systems,the likelihood is still relatively low.This is because we are able manage this risk through employing expert third party service providers for many of our critical IT systems.Through these third-party experts we can share or transfer the risk to those who have more expertise than ourselves in monitoring and managing this risk.The expertise offered by these third-party suppliers should allow us to reduce the likelihood of this risk. Financial risks -Reduce Fuel currently accounts for over 20%of Corjetz's costs and therefore any significant fluctuation in this cost will impact on our bottom line.This is an issue for us because our shareholders are likely to be expecting steady growth and performance.Although short term volatility is expected and can be useful to make short term gains,in the long term,shareholders will not look favourably on our business if earnings are volatile.The risk assessment report indicates that political instability in the oil producing regions in the last three months has caused significant fluctuations in crude oil prices which would suggest that the likelihood of rising fuel prices willbe high. In terms of impact,it will certainly impact on our profitability,but it will not mean that the business operations will be disrupted and there should be no consequences for passengers in the short term.Therefore,the current impact can be assessed as low,therefore the TARA framework suggests that we should find risk strategies to 'Reduce' this risk.The most common way to reduce such a risk is through fuel hedging,a contractual tool that airlines use to reduce exposure to the volatility of fuel prices.We can hedge the risk of a possible increase in crude oil prices by purchasing forward contracts,which usually involves engaging in a contract where the parties agree to buy or sell a certain quantity of fuel at a specified price on a future date.This then gives us some degree of certainty to the price that will be paid and therefore reduces the risk of volatility. This risk is one which we must monitor closely,as should fuel price volatility continue,we may need to pass this on to customers in our ticket prices,which could impact on demand and reduce profitability.Therefore,the impact would move towards 'High'and therefore alternative risk management strategies would be needed. Climate risks-Accept As noted in the latest risk assessment report,climate change is an increasing threat to the airline industry,both in terms of potentially increasing costs due to regulation and legislation demands and due to its impact on the climate and the adverse weather conditions this creates.In terms on the consequences/impact on our overall operations,these are currently relatively low.Adverse weather is an increasing issue for all airlines,but it currently does not have significant operational impact on the whole business.It could be disruptive in certain parts of our region but overall,most operations are likely to continue as normal.Legislation and regulatory changes relating to the climate are likely to continue to be a challenge but again,currently their impact on us is not significant. In terms of probability,this is most definitely increasing.We are aware of increasing legislation and increasing adverse weather events.However,in terms of likelihood of adverse conditions severely impacting our overall business operations,the probability is relatively low.Therefore,from the TARA matrix,we would accept this risk as a normal part of our business operations,meaning that we do not necessarily need to take specific risk mitigation action.However,as a responsible organisation,I would recommend that we do proactively monitor and manage this risk and find ways to manage our own impact on the changing climate.This is likely to mean working with governments and regulators to ensure we are working within recommended climate and emissions targets and that we actively monitor weather patterns. Security risks-Avoid The probability of an operational threat to our flights into Sondar is high,considering that civil unrest is increasing and the fact that one of our competitor's flights was hijacked only last week. Although we only fly a limited number of flights to Sondar,as a high-profile business based in Corland,this may be an opportunity for a hostile regime to attack the Corland government. The impact of such a threat occurring could be catastrophic.Although our competitors reported only injuries to crew and staff,there is no way of knowing that such attacks could escalate and the next case could be more dangerous.We cannot allow our staff and passengers to be placed at such risk.Therefore,as both likelihood and consequences are considered to be high,we should avoid this risk altogether and withdraw allservices to Sondar until the political situation stabilises and the threat of disruption becomes manageable.This risk cannot currently be taken. It is worth noting that security is a key risk to our business but in most cases,it is not one that we can avoid,as it is an inevitable aspect of the environment within which we operate.Therefore,in terms of our overallsecurity,we would try to reduce these risks as much as possible through multiple security measures,including robust security systems operating both in-flight and on the ground.We should also transfer much of this risk to industry experts in airport security who operate through the airports that we fly to. I hope that this email is informative and useful for your meeting. The answer was presented in a suitable email format. 2.66 401.33 2.66 4 0 Requirement 3(a) To: Corjetz's shareholders From: Senior business analyst Date: September 20X3 Briefing Paper Technological Developments Part 1:Benefits and challenges of data analytics Corjetz applies data analytics extensively throughout the business and considers the data that it generates as a key business resource.We should use this data to help us to maintain our strategic position as an efficient,low-cost airline operator. Benefits · Big data servers allow Corjetz to capture and interrogate on-board flight data relating to the operational performance of the flight and to interrogate the on-board maintenance systems.By capturing in-flight performance data from every flight,Corjetz can identify patterns of performance across the whole fleet to enable potential issues to be spotted early,allowing for early intervention before serious technical faults are developed.This will save costly delays in grounding aircraft when faults are potentially discovered later and could require more costly rectification work.It also ensures that there is limited disruption to schedules and customers, ensuring that we maintain customer satisfaction. · Capturing data from the aircraft maintenance system also helps Corjetz to predict when and where faults are most likely.This will help in being aware and prepared for these events and having alternative aircraft ready and/or having spare parts or necessary technical experts on hand and ready exactly when they are needed.This should reduce down-time and minimise the costs associated with customer disruption. · By using data analytics on our customer data,Corjetz can identify the best performing flights in terms of revenue per seat,load factor and customer spend and therefore we can try to increase the load factor of these flights or potentially update flight schedules to increase the frequency of the most profitable flights. · Analysis of on-board purchasing data willallow us to understand customer buying habits and predict what they will buy on-board.This will give us an opportunity to streamline the on-board service,reducing those items least purchased and ensuring that popular items are well stocked and advertised adequately.This should also minimise waste. · Analysis of how and where customers book their flights will give us the opportunity to target our marketing more effectively.Profiling customer types to certain destinations and flights will also help to streamline our marketing efforts and target customers more effectively through the most effective media channels.This should also improve customer satisfaction. · Effective scheduling(i.e.,offering the right flights to the right places at the right time)is a key component of our business model and data analytics can help us in this process.By being able to predict flights most likely delayed we can put strategies in place to ensure these are minimised,such as working more closely with affected airports and by re-scheduling flights to avoid times when delays are most likely to occur,thus minimising the costs associated with delays. ·Analysis of crew locations will assist us in optimising crew rotas and locations to ensure cabin crews are optimised ensuring we are optimising staff usage. Challenges · Big data analytics is highly complex.As an organisation,we are collecting millions of data transactions every day about each aircraft,in terms of its operational performance,in flight statistics,engine performance and maintenance data.In addition to that we collect allthe customer transaction data both pre-and in-flight.It is evident therefore that there is a potential for data overload. · Too much information could be as dangerous as not having enough to make effective strategic decisions.So much information could in fact stifle speed of response to time critical operational decisions.Data analysis could be a distraction to our core business if we spend too long analysing the data we collect without meaningful results. · Data analytics requires expertise to interrogate and understand the data effectively.The sheer quantity of data could easily be misinterpreted,and incorrect conclusions drawn from the analysis.Data analytic experts are expensive to train and expensive to retain in the business,as competitors are likely to be challenging to obtain the same expertise. · Collecting,managing and analysing vast quantities of data requires extensive expenditure not only on the experts described above but also the technological systems to collect and store this data.This cost will need to be carefully weighed against the opportunities described above. · Systems security is also a key challenge.We will be holding large volumes of customer specific data and we will be obliged by law to maintain and control this information on behalf of our customers.Such information is an obvious target for malicious attack or espionage and therefore it will be critical to maintain and operate the highest levels of systems security.Any breach could be seriously damaging to our reputation and costly in terms of compensation payments and regulatory fines. Overall,it is likely that the benefits gained from being able to exploit the vast amount of data we hold in our business to support and strengthen our strategic position can outweigh the potential challenges of data analytics. Briefing Paper(continued) Part 2:Potential impact of disruptive technologies on Corjetz It is anticipated that the airline industry will continue to face challenges and opportunities from emerging and disruptive technologies.There are several types of emerging technologies that Corjetz could exploit to support its strategic position. Advanced Robotics There are two potential robotics technologies that we could exploit.Firstly,the customer assistance robots could have two impacts.Firstly,it could enhance customer service at the airports where we operate by providing customers with additional advice and information to make their transit through the airport easier.This should improve customer service.However,we would need to take into consideration the potential for customer resistance to this impersonal approach to customer service delivery and we would need to assess which locations this type of technology would be best suited in terms of customer demographics.From the perspective of Corjetz,we would be able to potentially reduce some of our staffing costs by replacing the ground airport customer facing staff with robots. Pre-flight safety checks of all aircraft before departure is an integral part of flight safety activities. Application of robotic aircraft maintenance systems would enable external aircraft pre-flight checks to be undertaken.This would reduce the number of on-ground maintenance crew that Corjetz would need to employ at each airport.This would reduce the costs associated with operating maintenance crews and would potentially reduce the time taken to undertake external aircraft checks as these would be routinised and pre-programmed and carried out without human distractions.Therefore,this could help to reduce potential delays.However,human ground maintenance staff would still be required to facilitate the robotics and to rectify any issues found by the robots. Internet of Things (loT) The loT is the concept of connecting any device to the Internet and then to other connected devices.This connected network collects and shares data about the way each connected device/ object is used and about the environment around them.The loT platform integrates data from the different devices and applies analytics to share the most valuable information to detect patterns, make recommendations,and detect possible problems before they occur. One possible application for Corjetz is in improving logistics of spare parts.By fitting sensors to the most critical aircraft parts,we would be able to have real time data about performance of these parts and detect when they need replacing.This would be linked directly to key suppliers who could be notified of the need for parts in advance to ensure that waiting times for maintenance/ replacement are reduced or removed completely.This would reduce aircraft downtime and reduce potential costly delays to flights or additional payments to suppliers for emergency or unexpected supply requirements. Another application of loT is in weather forecasting,whereby radar systems can be connected directly to aircraft to give pilots real time weather conditions.This should help Corjetz with route planning and being able to respond more quickly to customers in relation to up to date information on potential delays or re-scheduling.This should improve customer experience and customer service. Artificial Intelligence(AI) Using Al technology means pilots can now be trained partially using Al simulators.As an organisation that employs over 2,000 pilots,training costs are a significant expense both in terms of money and time.Al technology could have the potential to reduce the time taken to train pilots, therefore allowing us to optimise their time availability This could also have an impact on reducing our pilot training costs.However,we would need to look closely into this technology as any reduction in quality of training would impact on the quality of our service and the trust of our customers. By aligning artificial Intelligence(AI)with our marketing platforms,we can direct our marketing budget more effectively to specific channels to attract a specific customer group.Tailored marketing messages can be sent directly to individual customers based upon previous purchasing profiles,which in turn should help to stimulate further expenditure.This targeted marketing approach will assist us in retaining current customers and identifying marketing channels to promote our business most effectively to potential customers within the correct location.This will improve efficiency of marketing spend and retain customers and improve customer satisfaction. Corjetz headline results and cost breakdow 1 1.Introduction Corjetz is a low-cost regional airline business.It was established 20 years ago in Corland,a country located in the continent of Hundria.Over the years,it has positioned itself as one of the world's leading low-cost airlines and is currently the second largest low-cost airline business operating in Hundria(in terms of passenger numbers). Corjetz currently operates nearly 1,000 routes (destinations)in over 30 countries across Hundria. Its head office is based at Near City airport,located in the south of Corland.Hundria is a politically stable and economically developed continent of the world.Most of the countries in Hundria form a trading bloc which facilitates free trade and free movement of people within it.Each country in Hundria uses its own currency.Corland's currency is the $. Corjetz operates a 'point-to-point'service,meaning that it operates its flights directly between destinations.This means that its passengers do not have to travel to or change flights at a 'hub' airport to reach their destination,which helps Corjetz to reduce its operating costs.It also keeps costs low through its 'no-frills'approach to flight delivery.This approach means that in-flight meals are not provided (unless paid for separately)and includes the automation of a wide range of its processes,including bookings,flight documentation,baggage drop and flight check-in. Additionally,it operates a profitable and popular portfolio of routes between over 130 airports across Hundria. 2.Airline industry information Overview The airline industry encompasses a wide range of businesses called airlines which offer an air transport service to customers.Normally referred to as commercial airlines,these businesses offer scheduled commercial flights which carry both human passengers and cargo, and in most cases are carried out using jet aircrafts.Commercial flights are those which transport passengers from one destination to another with a pre-booked ticket.Non-commercial flights are either operated privately or by the military. The airline industry forms just one sector of the wider aviation industry, which also includes all other aviation-related businesses,such as jet engine suppliers,aircraft manufacturers and aerospace companies. The airline industry directly and indirectly supports global trade,tourism,and economic growth. Therefore,it has had a major influence on globalisation in many other industries. Passenger types A leisure passenger is someone travelling on holiday to stay in a hotel or a holiday resort to relax and/or experience local tourist attractions.A business passenger is someone travelling for work purposes and will normally be attending a business meeting or conference.Both types of passengers may be offered the option to travel first class,business class or economy class by some airlines. First class passengers pay significantly more for their ticket but in return receive a high-quality service,including dedicated airport lounges,large and comfortable in-flight seats and unlimited food and drink provided throughout the flight.Business class passengers pay less than first class passengers for their tickets and receive a slightly lower level of service.Economy class passengers pay even less for their flight ticket than business class passengers.An economy price ticket normally includes a standard seat with limited leg room and standard and limited in-flight food options. Airline types-by service International airlines International airlines are the largest and often the most well-known airlines. International airlines offer global services,carrying leisure and business passengers and cargo over long distances.International airlines usually have multiple airport hubs which provide customers with access to hundreds of global destinations. National airlines National airlines typically focus on offering services within their home country,but many also offeraccess to international destinations.A national airline is likely to have a smaller aircraft fleet than the international airlines and often also offer services to both leisure and business passengers. Regional airlines Regional airlines tend to focus on offering services within specific regions, for example,a continent.In some cases,they provide passenger services to locations not offered by either national or international airlines. Airline types-by business model Full-service carriers Full-service carriers offer a range of pre-flight and on-board services within the price of the ticket.This may include checked-in baggage, in-flight meals,airport lounges and multiple service classes,such as first class,business class and economy class.They usually fly national, international,long-haul,and short-haul flights. Low-cost carriers Low-cost carriers aim to gain a competitive advantage over full-service carriers by reducing costs.This is done in several ways,including having smaller fleets and smaller aircraft,by offering a more limited service and by flying to smaller and less popular airports,where landing and ground handling fees are cheaper. Forcustomers,flights from low-cost carriers are often significantly cheaper. Services such as in-flight meals and baggage allowances willnormally not be included in the price of the ticket.Instead,where these facilities are available,customers pay extra for them.Some low-cost carriers offer both economy and business class services. Charter airlines Charter airlines focus on providing passenger services to tourists to a specific destination.In most cases,they do not sell airline tickets directly but instead enter into agreements with tour operators who then take responsibility for finding passengers for them. Cargo airlines The main focus of cargo airlines is the transportation of cargo or freight. Some cargo airlines are subsidiaries or sub-divisions of airlines which also offer passenger services. Hub and spoke versus point-to-point travel Airlines which operate a hub and spoke model fly from one airport into a hub airport,with passengers then connecting from the hub onto a flight to their final destination.Airlines operate this model because it allows them to offer more flights to passengers and is designed to optimise an airline's network coverage.The alternative to this is the point-to-point model,where the airline focuses on providing passengers with a route from origin to final destination.In this model,there is no need to connect passengers through a hub airport,as flights operate directly between cities.This model has proved popular with airline customers in recent years and most of the low-cost airlines operate this model. Key air transport statistics 20X2 Key statistics People transported(passengers per year) Average number of commercial flights per day Passenger revenues HUB-AND-SPOKE HUB-AND-SPOKE 20X2 Global 3 billion 73,000 $390 billion 20X2 Hundria 600 million 15,000 $79·2 billion Flight management activities Flight management activities are provided by airports.Airlines pay for these services via airport fees. Air traffic control (ATC) Air traffic control (ATC)is a vital aspect of air transportation.It is carried out by ground-based personnel specially trained in monitoring air traffic in a specific location.ATC is responsible for moving aircraft safely and efficiently through the airspace system.Air traffic controllers ensure that aircraft remain at set distances apart whilst moving them between airports along set flight paths. Aircraft ground handling Ground handling refers to the wide range of services provided to facilitate passengers within an airport and to facilitate an aircraft's ground positioning both before and after a flight.Ground handling services include: - Baggage management-including sorting passenger baggage and loading and unloading baggage onto and off aircraft - Customer service management -including gate assignment,and pre-flight facilities management (such as operating shops and waiting areas in airports). - Aircraft boarding management -including operating shuttle bus services between the airport and the aircraft,ramp facilities and passenger travel documentation management. - Aircraft towing and re-fuelling services -including movement of an aircraft to or from a parking stand or from one gate to another and the re-fuelling of aircrafts using fuel purchased by the airline. Airport security-including pre-flight passenger screening and security checks,baggage scanning,passport control and all on-site security management activities carried out to protect airport buildings,runways,and machinery. Industry challenges Demand is cyclical,with demand for flights driven by economic growth.In the last ten years, low-cost carriers have increasingly taken market share from full-service carriers by offering passengers affordable and low-cost air travel.This has made air travel more accessible to a much wider customer base globally.It has also introduced a high level of competition to the industry. The airline industry has been impacted by several geopolitical events in recent years,such as terror attacks,cyber-attacks and extreme weather events caused by climate change.These have led to both short and long-term consequences for demand.Terror and cyber-attacks have impacted on customer confidence in the industry and climate change is increasingly prominent on the global political agenda. Fuel is one of the biggest costs for airlines,and one of the most volatile.Fuel represents over 20% of most airlines'cost base annually.The price of jet fuel is strongly correlated to the international price of crude oil which has been highly volatile in the last five years.Fuel costs are particularly prone to foreign exchange movements,which can also have a significant impact on this major cost. The airline industry is still some way away from operating aircraft with alternative fuels such as biofuels,and most airlines are still fully reliant on crude oil.Governments and customers are becoming increasingly environmentally sensitive and the expectation for airlines to operate in a 'greener'way is increasing.If the airline industry is to survive in the long term,it will need to invest significantly in alternative fuel sources. Environmental factors are a major and increasingly significant issue for the airline industry, affecting both passenger demand and operating parameters.Customers are increasingly aware that their carbon footprint from airline travel is contributing around 3%of global carbon emissions. Therefore,many are actively considering alternatives to air travel. Despite environmental concerns,commercial international air traffic(aircraft operating in the skies)increased by 5%in 20X2.According to the latest industry data,the international air traffic control environment is struggling to cope with increasing flight numbers,evidenced by the number of delayed flights rising by nearly 20%in 20X2.Similarly,airports around the world are struggling to cope with demand from airlines for flight slots¹.This is leading to increased congestion at airports,resulting in more delays for passengers.This is also driving up aircraft ground handling service charges for all airlines. 1Aflight slot is the authorisation to either land or take off at a particular airport on a particular day during a specified time period.Flight slots are paid for by the airlines. Industry key performance indicators(KPls) Airlines use multiple performance metrics to measure and monitor their performance. Some examples are provided in the table below: KPI Definition 1.Available seat kilometres(ASK) ASK is calculated by taking the number of seats available and multiplying by the distance flown.It is a measure of airline capacity. 2.Revenue per available seat kilometre(RASK) RASK is calculated by dividing the airline's total revenue by its total ASK.It is a measure of potential revenue generation. 3.Load factor Calculated as the percentage of checked-in passengers in relation to an aircraft's available seats.It is a measure of utilisation. 4.Revenue passenger kilometres(RPK) RPK is calculated as the number of passengers multiplied by the total distance travelled.It shows the number of kilometres travelled by paying passengers who are referred to as 'revenue passengers'. 5.CO₂per passenger (grams) Calculated as total CO₂produced by the airline divided by the number of passengers. 6.Punctuality The percentage of flights which land on time at the destination airport.Typically,this means landing within 15 minutes of the scheduled arrival time. Industry body The airline industry has a global trade association.Its role is to help formulate industry policy and support airline activity.It promotes awareness of the benefits which the airline industry brings to national and global economies.Its role is also to develop global standards for the air transport industry,and it provides advice on airline safety and security by setting clearly defined rules. 3.Corjetz overview Background Corjetz was established in Corland 20 years ago by entrepreneurial brothers Eric and Axel Madson.They both had worked for many years in the airline industry and had a keen interest in travelling.With money inherited from their parents,they acquired two second-hand aircraft.They started by operating flights from Near City airport,located 40 miles from Corland's capital city in the south of the country,to airports located close to two cities in the north of the country. Using their knowledge and experience of the airline industry,Eric and Axel spotted a gap in the market in Corland for more convenient and affordable low-cost air travel.Up to that point, Corland's airline industry was dominated by full-service carriers. The low-cost business model in the Corland airline industry proved extremely popular with air passengers,who liked the convenience of Corjetz's point-to-point service and the significantly cheaper ticket prices than those offered by existing full-service carriers. Over the years,Corjetz has focused on operating flights to and from airports across Hundria with the highest customer demand and continually looks for opportunities to extend its network.Cost efficiency has been its overriding strategic focus.This has been delivered through developing and maintaining long-term strategic partnerships with aircraft manufacturers,key airports,ground handling operators and maintenance contractors who maintain Corjetz's owned aircrafts,and by operating the latest and most efficient aircraft.Low cost airline market shareHundria 20X2(passenger numbers)Brygair(Broland) 17%Corjetz(Corland)14%LKK(Lakland)9%Over the last 20 years the low-costmodel of air travel has continuedto be very popular with customersacross Hundria,and there are now 30low-cost airlines operating across thecontinent.Others(27 airlines)60% operating the latest and most efficient aircraft. Low cost airline market share Hundria 20X2(passenger numbers) Brygair (Broland) 17% Corjetz (Corland) 14% LKK (Lakland) 9% Over the last 20 years the low-cost model of air travel has continued to be very popular with customers across Hundria,and there are now 30 low-cost airlines operating across the continent. Others (27 airlines) 60% Eric and Axel both retired from the business 10 years ago but since their departure,Corjetz has remained focused on maintaining and strengthening its low-cost strategic position. Corjetz growth Routes(Destinations) 300 100 10 15 20 Ownership Corjetz was listed on the Corland stock market nine years ago,one year after Eric and Axel retired.The brothers originally retained a 35%share in the business when it was first listed but since then,they have sold most of their shareholdings in Corjetz and they no longer play any role in running the business.Currently,80%of Corjetz's shares are held by large institutional investors,10%of shares are held by Corjetz employees and the remaining shares are held by small private investors.Magda Fisk is the current CEO of Corjetz and has been in place for 10 years.She was instrumental in achieving Corjetz's listing. Corjetz key resources Routes Corjetz has a profitable and extensive portfolio of routes which it operates to and from over 130 airports across Hundria.This makes it very popular with customers,who find the range of destinations and frequency of flights offered by Corjetz very convenient and attractive.It regularly reviews its route network to exploit the latest demand opportunities. Fleet Corjetz operates a modern fleet of 300 aircraft,of which 75%are owned outright and the remaining 25%are leased (leasing is a common form of financing in the airline industry).Corjetz has invested significantly in the latest aircraft which are more fuel efficient and environmentally friendly.This has contributed significantly to its low-cost strategic position and has also positively impacted on reducing carbon emissions.Corjetz's average aircraft is 4·5 years old,which is low compared to the industry standard. Corjetz focuses on leisure passengers rather than on business passengers,therefore its aircraft cabins consist of economy class seats only with a high-density layout (this gives Corjetz 150 seats per aircraft compared to 140 seats on similar sized aircraft operated by its competitors). This higher seat density allows Corjetz to offer lower ticket prices per flight which is attractive to customers. Technology Corjetz applies data analytics extensively throughout its business and considers data to be a key business resource.It has always been an early adopter of the latest technologies in the airline industry.It currently makes extensive use of technology throughout its business operations, including: 一 A mobile app which allows passengers to book flights,check in for lights,download boarding passes (to either print off or store as a mobile boarding pass on the passenger's smartphone)and receive real-time flight status information.The app significantly reduces the cost of issuing,distributing and processing physical tickets.An enhanced feature of the mobile app is the integration of live data from the airport's information systems with the passenger's booking and flight details,allowing personalised instructions and updates to be sent to passengers before their journey. In most airports Corjetz operates fully automated baggage check-in machines to help reduce check-in queues. All aircraft are fitted with electronic touchpads for the crew,to replace laptops and printed navigational charts for pilots.This has replaced the need for each flight to carry heavy printed flight logbooks which has helped to reduce CO₂emissions per flight. All Corjetz's aircraft are fitted with big data servers to collect continuous in-flight data about flight and engine performance. Corjetz continually searches fornew technologies to enhance and supportits business operations, to improve efficiency and reduce costs. People Corjetz employs nearly 11,800 staff,including over 2,000 pilots and 6,500 cabin crew.The remaining staff fulfila number of roles,including staff operating at airports and head office staff operating in IT,finance,customer services and human resource management.Regular updates are provided to employees on key business issues which include a regularly updated intranet, a monthly ‘meet the board'online chat,and a weekly staff newsletter.All Corjetz employees undergo a full induction programme and have access to online learning resources,as well as career development opportunities.All employees receive regular and scheduled managerial appraisal of their performance. Board structure Corjetz has six executive directors and five NEDs(including the chair).The board structure is as follows: Non-executive chairSeniorNon-executive directorChief people officerNon-executive directorNon-executive directorChief finance officerChiefexecutive officerChiefinformationChiefcommercialChiefmarketingindependentnon-executivedirectorofficerofficerofficer Non-executive chair Senior Non-executive director Chief people officer Non-executive director Non-executive director Chief finance officer Chief executive officer Chief information Chief commercial Chief marketing independent non-executive director officer officer officer There are four sub-committees: 一 Audit committee Nomination committee Remuneration committee Safety committee Risk management and key risks Corjetz has a detailed risk register,and a robust enterprise risk management(ERM)framework is in place.The board takes responsibility for determining the nature and extent of the risks it is willing to take to achieve its strategic objectives.It is also responsible for maintaining and monitoring the systems of internal control and risk management.Risk management activities are co-ordinated by the risk and assurance team,led by a risk manager who reports to both the chief financial officer,and to the chair of the audit committee.Corjetz's key risks include: - Safety and security of customers and staff.Delivering safe and secure operations which meet the needs and expectations of customers and staff is critical to the business. - The growing impact of climate change and increasing expectations from customers, governments,regulators,and wider society for a greater focus on the environment and sustainability activities. - Macro-economic and geopolitical events such as general economic trends,foreign exchange rates and volatile fuel prices which all impact financial performance. - The availability,security and performance of its website and its critical business operating systems. 4.Corjetz website extracts What we do What we do We consistently deliver low fares to our customers and operate flights to the places our customers most want to travel to,using our cost advantage and leading position at airports across Hundria. These are,of course,important but they are not the outcomes which the transport minister emphasised and with which CC will therefore be associated. Even if failure to meet the outcomes which the transport minister stated does not affect the revenue CC obtains from the contract,it may face damage to its reputation. Some of the expectations for the new road whch the press release has encouraged may be unrealistic.It is not clear, for example,how the figure of reducing journey time by almost half was estimated.Natura constraints in the Isnardi mountains may mean that it is impossible for CC to build a road which can produce that level of reduction.Both the road and CC will also be judged by how much the road alleviates traffic jams.CC may be able to do nothing about this, as traffic on existing roads switching to the new road may just transfer the problem of congestion onto the new road. 16

(2 marks) (b)A summary forCC's board to review assessing the control weaknesses discussed at the emergency meeting, stating foreach control weakness its consequence and recommendation for improvements. (14 marks) Professional skills marks are available fordemonstrating evaluation skilsby basing explanations of significant control weaknessesonthe infomation provided and recommending appropriate improvements. The realities of child-labour Cocoa is a commodity crop grown primarily for export.As the chocolate industry has grown overthe last 20 years,so has the demand for cheap cocoa.However,on average,cocoa farmers in Geeland and Rodia earn less than $2 per day, an income significantly below the poverty line.Asa result,they often resort to the use of child-labour to keep prices competitive. The children of Geeland and Rodia are surrounded by intense poverty,and most begin working at a young age to help support their families and many offer vital sourcesof income for the poorest families.Children ae mostly used to climb the cocoa trees to cut bean pods using a machete.These large,heavy,dangerous knives are the standard tools for children on the cocoa farms,which violates international labour laws relating to eliminating the worst forms of child-labour.In addition,over 40%of child-labourers in Geeland and 60%in Rodia working on cocoa farms do not attend school. Is child-labour free chocolate possible? Despite their role in contributng to child-labour,the chocolate industry has not taken significant steps to remedy the problem.Within this billion dollar industry,chocolate companies have the power to end the use of chld-labour by paying cocoa farmers a living wage for their product.The chocolate industry is also being called upon to develop and financially support programmes to educate children working on cocoa farms.However,to date,the industry has seemingly achieved limited success in removing child-labour and a lack of transparencyis characteristic of the chocolate industry as it consistently fails to act. The truth is that consumers today have no sure way of knowing if the chocolate they are buying involves the use of child- labour.Despite the fact that last month a Northland journalist investigating farms in Rodia(where Northland chocolate companies buy cocoa)filmed illegal child-labour practices,a recent public survey in Northland highlighted that less than 5%of customers were aware of child-labour practices in the chocolate manufacturing industry. Clearly, Northland chocolate manufacturers have a long way to go to address these unethical activities and until they do, the ethical standards of these manufacturers must be called into question. 批注[Golden33]: 童工牵扯到道德问题，法律问题，会 对相对应公司声誉造成及其严重的影响。如何杜绝这一 事件的发生需要委员会慎重探讨和考虑。 批注[Golden34]: 巧克力制造商要解决这些不道德的行 为还有很长的路要走，在他们做到这一点之前，这些制 造商的道德标准可能会受到质疑。 PTO Exhibit6:Summary of brainstorming meeting Engagement with our customers: Regular email and promotional offers Customer surveys Staff training in customer engagement A variety of products to suit all price ranges Leading the business: Strong leadership team Supported by strong management team Mission and aims regularly reviewed Strong corporate governance What do we do to achieve performance excellence? high impact/high likelihood approach.The impact is high, particularly on our reputation and on the lives of those being exploited and the likelihood is also high.It is stated in the rticle that this is a widespread practice in the industry and therefore it is highly likely that some ofthe cocoa farmers a t who supply us do use child-labour.Therefore,the risk response advocated by the human resource director is to AVOID his risk,by finding alternative cocoa suppliers. The operations director's recommendation fits with low impact/low likelihood approach,in that he believes that the impact of this risk is low as most customers are unaware of child-labour practices.Therefore,the risk response his argument most closely follows is to ACCEPT this risk and take no action. The finance director's recommendation fits with the low impact/high likelihood approach,in that the risk is likely to be of low impact in the context of the industry-wide use of child-labour by cocoa farmers and the lack of awareness of its customers,but the likelihood is high,given the high number of cocoa farmers we use in Geeland and Roda.Therefore, the risk response would be to REDUCE this risk,through improved control activities Slide 2: Consideration of risk responses HRDIRECTOR AVOID - Unlikely high impact - High costs - Will not address the issue OPERATIONSDIRECTOR ACCEPT Notes: HR director AVOID To avoid the issue of child-labour is not the most suitable solution for Dulce. The newspaper article indicates the scale of the issue in the whole industry and,therefore,identifying and using cocoa farmers who do not use child-labour may be impractical,taking into account the large number ofcocoa supplies we need.There are advantages,in that it may attract anew range of customers who only buy products from guaranteed ethical sources but itis unlikely that these gains will off-set the cost of sourcing new cocoa farmers and the potential loss of customers due to likely price increases ofour products. The impact of this issue is not considered to be hgh,as most customers are not aware of these labour practices and are unlikely to change their buying behaviour if they did As chocolate is a low value product for consumers,this issue is unlikely to be a major factor in the purchasing decision o most customers. Overall,it would be better to remain working with these cocoa farmers to put in place controls to ensure child-labour practices are monitored and assistance is given to provide educationand support to both farmers and the children working on these farms.Therefore,the suggestion by the human resource director may not be the best solution to the risk. Operations director ACCEPT Although the use of child-labour in cocoa faming is common practice,that does not mean that we should merely accept it and not attempt to improve the situation._ Although the risk of child-labour which would be used by our cocoa farmers is likely to be of low impact on our overall businessfrom the evidence in the news article,it is likely to be of high likelihood and therefore the assertion by the operations director may not be justified Evidence suggested in the article indicates that the majority of the cocoa farms located in the two cocoa growing countries do use child-labour.Therefore,it would seem highly likely that some,if not a significant number of the cocoa farmers supplying our key suppliers,do use child-labour. Obviously,doing nothing will cost nothing and it means no change in what we do.However,lit goesdirectly against our stated beliefsas an organisation,as evidenced in the ethical sourcing statement ofour annual report.We also run the risk that ethical consumers in Northland will seek altemative chocolate suppliers.Our strategic aim is to develop and maintain strong customer relationships and this approach would likely go against this aim. performance excellence throughout our business. 21 ONLINE DELIVERY-PLATINUM ONLINE DELIVERY-PLATINUM 高顿 ONLINE DELIVERY-PLATINUMGOLDEN EDUCATION ONLINE DELIVERY-PLATINUM 真题解析2019-MarJun Background information SmartWear is a long established clothing retailer in Noria,a highly-developed northern European country,which is currently in the middle of a deep economic recession.The country is also experiencing political uncertainty because of its coalition government,who many influential commentators believe will not last for its full term of office. These two factors have contributed to record high levels of unemployment,low business confidence and a steady decline in the standard of living of the general population.The company also operates in two other countries on the European continent,Southland and Centrum. SmartWear has enjoyed long-term business success mainly due to the fact that it has carefully positioned itself towards the lower end of the clothing retail sector.It sources its products directly from suppliers in low-cost economies,and it has been able to target a mass market with consistently low prices.This has meant that during the recent economic downturn in Noria it has been able to profit from the growing number of price-sensitive consumers,who have switched from the more high-end of the clothing retail market to budget retailers like SmartWear-who are the market leader in this high volume market. SmartWear's long term strategy has always been based on a low cost- low price business model,which is described through its mission statement and strategic goals.However,following a prolonged period of expansion and growth,including successfully penetrating several other European markets,the company is for the first time experiencing a marked downturn in both its operational and financial performance. This appears to be in part due to economic pressure and the growing intensity of competition in all of its markets,both at home and abroad. At a recent board meeting to discuss the latest set of disappointing financial results,it was proposed that an external consultant be appointed to investigate the deteriorating performance of SmartWear and propose the best way forward for the company. 批注[1]:背景介绍 批注[2]: SmartWear 所处外部环境： 1.服装零售 in Noria 2.严重的经济衰退 3.政治环境不稳定：联合政府，且届满后可能不再继续 4.失业率高；经营信心弱；生活水平下降 批注[3]: SmartWear 能获得成功的原因： 1.定位准确：低端市场(低成本低价模式) 2.Market leader in 低端市场 3.吸引 price-sensitive customer(非常适合经济衰退时期) 批注[4]: SmartWear目前遇到的问题： 1.经济形势带来的压力 2.竞争变得更加激烈 3.SmartWear 快速扩张 4.目前经营和财务业绩出现下降 批注[5]:请外部咨询师调查业绩变差的原因，并且找到最 佳改善方案。 真题解析2019-MarJun 2 You are the external consultant engaged by SmartWear,and you have gathered the following information to help you with your assignment: Exhibit 1:Briefing notes prepared by the business development department for the consultant.
